constituency, and especially on the lovely town of SteTherese, the privilege of possessing a factory of such importance.Nobody will withhold from the present government his congratulations for having generously helped the various provinces to weather the unemployment crisis through which Canada is passing.It was a very urgent duty.I am unaware whether, in its grants to help the provinces to stem unemployment, the dominion government reserved the right to indicate how the money it supplied was to be spent or whether it intends to do so in the future, through the grants that it may allow them for such a purpose.Although my knowledge of the economic situation of the country is rather limited and that I do not feel qualified to suggest a specific solution, a sovereign and infallible remedy, I may state that in the province of Quebec and probably also in all the other provinces, though in an unequal measure, the solution of the serious problems of unemployment rests mostly in the return to the farm.I am convinced that our country is still first of all a farming one and that it is.The AddressMr.Bourgeois.y encouraging our farmers that we must begin our economic restoration.As Theodore Roosevelt stated:.he only effective way to help any man is to help him to help himself and the worst lessen to teach him is that he can be permanently helped at the expense of someone else.In a splendid novel which had a remarkable success in France and which was exceptionally well translated in English by our fellowcitizen, Sir Andrew MacPhailI refer to "Maria Chapdelaine"Louis Hemon drew the exquisite portrait of the young French Canadian girl, daughter of pioneers and future mother of forest settlers, an ever present picture, but, in many ways, this portrait is also that of many other young Canadian girls, whether they be from English, Scotch or Irish descent.However, "Maria Chapdelaine" must, to preserve her high ideals and physical beauty, her charm and smile, remain in the country and continue to live the ancestral life.The atmosphere of cities to her is unhealthy, she wastes away, and the same may be said of her cousins, the English, Scotch, Irish, or the French Canadians whom city life unnerves and weakens when it does not contaminate them physically or infect their minds with socialism or even worse, communism.Let us rest assured, there is no Hercules, no adversity capable of laying low the Canadian people, if following the example of Antaeus, their feet stick to the soil, their fostermother.I now broach the important question of our railways.One must be blind or in bad faith not to acknowledge that at present their condition is far from being satisfactory.By degrees, our three transcontinental railways were built across Canada:	the Canadian.acific, the Canadian Northern and the Grand Trunk Pacific.Did legitimate hopes justify the building and the completing of these three great railway systemsIn a speech delivered at the Sphinx Club, in London, the Hon.Rodolphe Lemieux, an exSpeaker of this house, after mentioning the existence in Canada of these three great transcontinental railways, predicted that it would soon be necessary to construct the Hudson Bay railway and, he further added:s, we are proud of our system of transportation, and only yesterday the Governor of Canada, one of the best men that the English aristocracy has ever given to Canada, Earl Grey, rediscovered the Hudson Bay which was known to tile old traders of the Hudson Bay Company two hundred years ago, the Hudson Bay through which our own Commissioner in London, Lord Strathcona, came nearly eighty years ago.When I was a lad, he left Scotland to serve Canada and to serve the Empire.Yes, Hudson Bay has been rediscovered.To show you the importance of the country, the importance of the foreign .nterest in Canada, the three transcontinentals will not be sufficient to give an outlet for transportation of our grain, but we shall require, in the near future, the shorter route of the Hudson Bay.All this will benefit Canada, but above all it will benefit the British Empire.But these four railwaysfor the Hudson Bay railway of which the Hon.Mr.Lemieux proclaimed the necessity, has also been built are almost all in a deplorable financial state.Does this, however, justify charging this government with a want of foresight or with negligence.No.These conditions of which we deplore the existence, arise from numerous causes.First, we must mention the worldwide disturbance brought on by the war of 1914.We must further take into account the closing of our country to immigrants from Europe and, on the other hand, take into consideration that these European countries and the Argentine republic as well, have been within these last years, growing wheat on a large scale.The consequence, therefore, was that on both these scores our railways were the losers.Less immigrants to transport to the west and less wheat to carry to our Atlantic ports.Add to these causes the competition of motor trucks.Must we for all that throw up the sponge and allow our railways, especially those that have become state lines, to extricate themselves as best they canI feel certain that the house holds different views, that it approves tihe policy of the present government which is to 'help our railways, and especially those which form part of our national inheritance.However, before further steps are taken, the government has decided to inquire into the conditions prevailing in these extensive systems, in order to remedy their defects, and that is why a commission of inquiry, presided over by Hon.Justice Duff, has been appointed.It is only reasonable to expect that tlhis inquiry will bring to light the shortcomings of our railway systems and point out the retrenchments to be made in their management.This house learns that our government has entered into negotiations with the United States of America for the purpose of carrying out the St.Lawrence deep waterway.I am not acquainted with the plans prepared in this connection by the engineers to whom the study of this colossal project has been entrusted.All I know is what has been disclosed to the public by the Canadian press.This deep waterway would be undertaken in that part of our great river comprised between Prescott and Cornwall known as the international section, and our rich neighbours, the United States, would contribute largely to this gigantic enterprise.The house feels confident.The AddressMr.Bourgeois.hat in the working out of this scheme, the government was guided solely by the interests of Canada; there can be no question in this matter of international courtesy.No one will gainsay that the possession of this great waterway on the greater part of its course, constitutes one of the major assets of this country, I would even go so far as to state that it is the main channel of our economic life.I am not an hydraulic engineer; however, I may state that from the geographical viewpoint, considering the area drained, the width and depth of its course and the enormous flow of water, the St.Lawrence is, without comparison, the most important waterway in the world.None can be compared to it, neither the Amazon, the Nile, the Volga and the Rhine.Pascal said: ''Rivers are movable roads. The St.Lawrence is an imperial highway which moves along carrying towards the ocean and Europe the products of the entire northern part of this continent.Over a thousand miles of its course, it is navigable for oceangoing vessels, so much so that at Montreal, we have this unique fact, an ocean port located a thousand miles from the ocean.I represent a constituency of the province of Quebec, and so far as electric energy is concernedfor it is certainly with this purpose in view that the deep waterways of the St.Lawrence is soughtQuebec possesses all the water power it needs.Therefore, it is not as a citizen of the province of Quebec that I am looking into this problem.I consider that such a vast undertaking, one of such a vital interest to the country, should be studied without losing sight of the interests of the Dominion as a whole, and it is not simply as a FrenehCanadian but solely as a Canadian, one dyed in the wool, that I consider this matter.We have the firm hope that this great enterprise of the St.Lawrence deep waterway will be carried out in the interest and to the advantage of Canada.This faith rests on the confidence we are reposing, and with good reason, on the patriotism of our Prime Minister and his colleagues.We shall not be deceived, I feel absolutely convinced of this, because they are fully aware of their responsibilities as heirs and followers of the policy laid down by Macdonald and Cartier, they are endowed with the courage and foresight necessary to see that nothing is sacrificed of our interests or nothing is neglected in order to safeguard our rights.The speech from the throne mentions the Conference on disarmament opened at Geneva on February 2 last.Let us hope that our representatives will work efficiently to establish a lasting peace throughout the world, and.hus promote the prosperity and greatness of the British Empire and, thereby, of Canada which forms part of it.May I now refer to the Imperial Economic Conference, which is to take place at Ottawa, on July 18, next.We are entitled to expect much good from it.This Conference, without doubt, will be one of the most important events in our country's history.The ideas advocated in London by the Prime Minister, in October, 1930, are, we are convinced, on the eve of being entirely put into practice, to the best advantage of Canada and the other dominions forming part of the British Commonwealth.The various and numerous proposals announced in the speech from the throne lead us to believe that we shall have, during the present session, to fulfil an arduous task.Might I be allowed to wish everyone, in the fulfilment of this task, the indomitable courage, the invincible confidence and heroic tenacity which was shown by one of the most illustrious inhabitants of this Canadian soil.We have in Three Rivers, in that city almost three centuries old, since it was founded in 1634 by the Sieur de Laviolette, that historical and glorious town of Three Rivers which I have the great privilege of representing here, we have, I say, a humble monument which narrates one of the most thrilling feats of our national history.Along our esplanade, skirting the St.Lawrence river, there is to be found a pedestal upon which the wayfarer reads with emotion:.n this site was located the house where was born, November 17, 1685, the most illustrious son of Three Rivers.Pierre Gaultier de Varennes, Sieur de la Verendrye, the discoverer of the Rocky mountains and the Canadian West.After untold hardships and at the cost of superhuman efforts, La Verendrye, with his own resources only, without the help of any governing power, started from the post of Ivaministikwia and went up this river, skirting Rainy Lake, where he built in 1731, Fort St.Pierre then proceeding along lake of the Woods, where he erected, the following year Fort St.Charles, he continued along the Winnipeg river, where he build in 1734, Fort de Maurepas.Crossing towards the northwest, lake Dauphin (Manitoba) and lake du Cygne, he explored the Biche (Red Deer) river and reached the source of the Saskatchewan river.In 1738, he erected Fort LaReine (Portage la Prairie) on the north shore of the Assiniboia river and, in the angle formed by this river and that of the Red river, he erected Fort.The AddressMr.Bourgeois.

subsubtopic: ALLOTTED DAY, S.O. 82-ALLEGED LACK OF GOVERNMENT CONSIDERATION FOR YOUTH
clean_text: That this House condemns the Government for its lack of consideration for Canada's youth, for its inaction in the face of the crisis an entire generation of Canadian men and women are faced with, and for neglecting to take immediate action in order to allow them to participate fully in Canadian society.He said: Mr.Speaker, I welcome this opportunity to speak to the House today, at a time when the youth question has captured the attention of many Canadians but, unfortunately, has not sufficiently captured that of the Government.That is why we, the Official Opposition, want to dedicate this Opposition Day to making the Government and all Members of this House aware of the very serious situation we have in Canada today.Mr.Speaker, at this very moment, there are 700,000 young Canadian men and women who, although they want to participate fully in society, unfortunately have no opportunity to do so.We have 700,000 young people who are asking society for a small place in the sun, who really want to work, and they are being told outright: Sorry, no room.We have these 700,000 individuals who are losing all their illusions and who, in many cases, have already lost hope, because after the sacrifices they made to get an education, their hopes and dreams have now been dashed by the current situation and by the state of the economy.Today, I thought it was essential for the Government, and in this respect, I do deplore the absence of the Minister of State (Youth) (Mrs.Champagne).She seldom has an opportunity to take part in the proceedings, and she is probably waiting for someone to write her speech so she can rehearse it, like a script.Meanwhile, I am sorry she is not here in the House to discuss with us the problems of Canada's youth.Mr.Speaker, I think all Canadian men and women will realize that we, as parliamentarians and with our responsibility for the wellbeing of Canadians, should ask ourselves a number of questions and do some soulsearching.We should ask ourselves whether as elected representatives, we have done enough to make equal opportunity a reality for young Canadians.And if during the recession, we merely did what we could to save what we already had, we must remember that those who had nothing were unable to enter the system.We should also ask ourselves whether we are parliamentarians and especially you as the Government have fulfilled the mandate you were given by Canadians.After looking at the statistics and reading various papers published by the Government, we realized that since September 4, 1984, when this.arch 12, 1986.Government received a majority of support from young Canadians, they were the first group this Government decided to drop.And it did so for a very simple reason.This group does not have a powerful lobby.It is not one of Frank Moore's customers.It is not..in fact, it is isolated in so many ways, Mr.Speaker.And of course this Government is ignoring the isolated members of this group, each of whom is going through some particularly dramatic situation.The Minister of State (Youth) has buried her head in the sand ever since her appointment.She would not face reality.She thought that she had been appointed to speak on behalf of all those who had succeeded, to strut about, to try and convince everybody that everything was fine and dandy in the best possible world, and to avoid at all cost to address the needy.As you know, Mr.Speaker, she has defended so weakly her constituency that she has been divested entirely of her own department and budget.The Minister of State (Youth) is here today before us with the only support of her chauffeur and limousine.Under these circumstances, I do not think that she can do much for her constituency who need help so very much.When we look at the situation straight in the face, Mr.Speaker, we realize that it is a tragedy.The unemployment level is very high and, as usual, the Prime Minister will provide us with a whole series of figures which the Minister of State (Youth) will repeat.But the whole situation is much more serious than these figures would indicate.They say the unemployment level is lower than it was in September 1984, but youth unemployment increased last month, something nobody mentioned in the House.And the tendency is not downwards.I dare hope that the Minister of State (Youth) will realize for instance that in Nova Scotia, the unemployment level among young Canadians is . per cent higher than it was when she assumed her portfolio.It has also increased in New Brunswick since then.It is all very nice to generalize, but there are particular situations.When we read the Nielsen report and realize that businessmen are now controlling this Government, we can imagine what is happening to the poor devils who are 16, 17 or 18 years of age.They cannot influence the Government.They are represented by a Minister who was supposed to defend them and, now that she is on her way out, can hardly defend herself.They have a Minister who wanted to manipulate them.Her own correspondance bears witness to that, Mr.Speaker.She is more interested in the future of the Federation of Young Conservatives than in the lot of young Canadians generally.Mr.Speaker, I feel that it is scandalous for her to want to.pend $7. million of taxpayers' money to get young people to.oin the Progressive Conservative Partythe Minister con.upply.essed that much herselfwhile fully ignoring the dramatic situation of young Canadians generally.Mr.Speaker, the Minister of State (Youth) confessed her helplessness in her letter dated January 6, 1986.She recognized having wasted $100 million in the Youth programs since her election.If this is being concerned about the situation of young Canadians and being part of a Government eager to meet their needs, Mr.Speaker, I cannot understand how the Minister of State (Youth) can claim today having done a good job over the past year and a half.Mr.Speaker, looking back at what has been going on these last few days, there was, of course, that famous letter from the Minister, but there was also the spectacular, sad and dramatic gesture by our colleague from the Upper House, Senator Jacques Hebert.And that gesture, Mr.Speaker, is a symbol of how serious the situation is.This does not happen every day, and indeed I think it is the first time in history that a Member of this Parliament has decided to offer himself, his health in fact to try and wake up the bunch of sleepyheads in the Government.Mr.Speaker, I see some of the Members shaking their heads.But I do not expect one single Member in that party to have the courage to defend a constituency with that much faith and devotion.And to that extent, Mr.Speaker, I feel the situation will force the Government to start thinking, because they have been laughing these last three days.I venture to predict that their laugh will turn sour as days go by because they will have the whole situation on their conscience.And then, I think that over the coming days, the media will keep focusing the general public's attention on the situation of our young.All of us in our constituencies are aware of instances of neighbours, of people we know who have not found jobs, whatever the statistics may say.We know that the Manpower Centres are filled with young people waiting in line for opportunities.It is therefore not good enough to be satisfied with the situation and refer to statistics.I do not understand why the Government does not admit its error now.They slashed through programs, in an arbitrary, hasty and somewhat vengeful way.But actually, it is not senator Hebert who is being hurt, but the thousands of young people who had pinned their hopes on that program.And the thousands of young people who are also waiting for the Government to respond to the Senate report.In the Senate report, Mr.Speaker, there are 26 recommendations.It is not a Liberal report, it is a unanimous Senate report, about which Conservatives senators have agreed with Libera] senators to say this: The situation is far past the stage of partisan considerations.And although that report was made public, we did not even hear a whistle from the Minister of State (Youth), as usual.However, the other Members of the Governement should take the time to read it, and act on it.March 12, 1986.upply.ecause what is in that report is a call from the heart, and it is my hope that this Government will have the courage to act within the shortest possible time.I suspect there is a move, I think that in the last few days they have been thinking.It started three days ago when the Deputy Prime Minister said: "We will do nothing, all we have been doing is very well done, hurrah for the statistics, and those who do not appear in the statistics, let them manage by themselves".Yesterday, the Prime Minister sent this reply to Senator Hebert, and in his letter the Prime Minister admitted: I would be the first to recognize that much more should be done for the young.That having been said, I think there is a move.Today the Prime Minister, probably on the way out of his caucus, said that probably, by the end of the month, the Government would be announcing new programs for the young.It would therefore seem that he is beginning to see the light.The Minister of State said she was not aware of anything.This is no news to us, she has never been aware of anything since she has been there, Mr.Speaker.I think she should inquire from the Prime Minister because it could help her in her speech today and she would look better if she did not contradict the Prime Minister before the end of the debate.I think it is important to know that at 12:23 p.. according to a Canadian Press report, the Prime Minister has said he will announce new programs before the end of the month.However, she may no longer be there and this is why he did not ask her advice.However, Mr.Speaker, I think we should go beyond the personal case of the Minister of State, because the Government will deal with it, but we should look after the young people.As a result of the situation in the Senate which no doubt will become embarrassing for the Government, I know of no Government who would face such a situation without doing a selfexamination and taking action.In that case, why should we waitIndeed, we know that a mistake was made when programs were swiftly cut.They admit that they have not done enough, that new measures will be taken before the end of the month but why are they not acting nowI think it would be in the interests of young Canadians.When the Government killed Katimavik, they promised that the $20 million would be transferred to another program and, of course, the Minister of State did not know what kind of program it would be.It would perhaps be proper now for the Minister of State to rise, to show some leadership and announce a new program which would end the human drama of certain young people throughout the country and would hopefully satisfy our colleague, senator Hebert.Since certain Members are making light of the situation in the Senate, I would like to ask them to reflect on an editorial in today's Le Droit:.he most sinister sniggering in the history of the House of Commons in Ottawa may have been heard on Monday.The whole venerable assembly of the representatives of the Canadian people once burst out laughing about the problem of battered women.When advised Monday about the fast of Senator.acques Hebert, who is asking for the Katimavik Program to be reestablished, the Conservative Members of Parliament did something even more ugly.The editorial goes on to say that, by laughing at Jacques Hebert and his hunger strike to save Katimavik, which represents the only ray of hope for many young people, the sniggering Conservatives showed their simplemindedness, their absence of morality, or even their pleasure at a situation which is in fact quite disastrous.There is no other reason to react as they did.I dare hope that the Minister of State (Youth) and all her colleagues will reflect upon these words written by editorialist Johannes Martin Godbout.Today the House is wondering what we as parliamentarians might do for that important segment of our population.It is a starting point.We will be told there is no money, think of the funds required and the deficit.How far can we go on hiding behind deficits and sacrificing an important part of a generationHow wrong can our priorities be when we can find $1 billion to bail out wealthy bank depositors and pretend there is nothing left to help the most dynamic forces in our societyAs suggested in the editorial, I think it is a lack of ethics.I know that this Conservative Government is not overly sensitive to the plight of young Canadians, except perhaps to offer them minor census jobs.My colleague from GlengarryPrescottRussell (Mr.Boudria) gave us a good example today when he told us that, at the request of the Minister of State (Youth), the Minister of Supply and Services (Mr.Mclnnes) followed her advice and offered census jobs to young people, particularly those who belong to the Ottawa University Conservative club.I think measures of this nature are not worthy of a proud Government or of parliamentarians like our colleague from Valleyfield who, I know, has a lot more class than that and is aware that the future of youth is a matter of honour and that we should not belittle the problems experienced by the new generation.I think that the Government is not offering them opportunities because it feels that those who are lucky enough will make out all right, but too bad about the others.The fact is that solutions are there, they have been proposed by various organizations.We have had studies galore.The one example I would give is the First Chance program which could have met urgent needs.The Minister of State (Youth) may laugh her head off! What to expectI think she has no sense of responsibility, because young people urgently need ready access to the labour market, they need practical experience.I would suggest that the First Chance program could have been one of many avenues open to them.Call it whatever you like, but do something!. think that it is important for young Canadians to feel that.ot only big business lobbies can influence this Government,.hat not only large companies, such as the oil multinationals.March 12, 1986..which have so much attention already, can catch the attention of the Government, but that ordinary people can do the same, that is those who cannot afford to attend weekend conventions or come to Ottawa to protest.I have been told: There are not that many youth organizations because there are no demonstrations on the Hill.Even coming here from Montreal or Toronto is too much to ask people who might have to live on $145 a month in welfare benefits.Would we want them to live on even lessI think that you only have to look at the facts to realize that there is a major problem.I therefore hope that the events of the last few days and the daily problems facing many young people will convince our friends opposite to stop being concerned only by those who make large contributions to the Party and to take care of real people.I hope that they will showand we already know that this is a big business Governmentthat this Government also has a heart, that it has courage and that it can admit that, because the Members opposite tried to go too fast in reducing the deficit, they forgot to take into account the human factor and to examine the type of situation in which 700,000 young people find themselves, they forgot to consider that these young people need at least the same opportunities as the other elements in our society.There is now a difference of 6 per cent in the unemployment rate for young people and the rate for older Canadians.Some positive action must therefore be taken to help these people.I do not see why this Government refuses to accept its responsibilities and I am convinced that the discussions that many Members opposite will have next weekend in their own constituency will give them pause and convince them to ask their caucus, their Leader and their Ministers to take more positive action in the coming weeks.I trust that this will happen sooner than later and that the signs shown by the Prime Minister these last few days will lead to the implementation of a major program, not only knickknacks, but something substantial, not just a political gesture to try not to lose face and show that something has been done, but a real program, a bold program, geared to the needs of young Canadians.I am convinced that, should you take a polland you do that often enoughyou would find that Canadians would be willing to make some sacrifices for the upcoming generation.I can say, right now, that all Canadians want to give young people a chance.Ask your own conscience, look at your own children.Don't you want them to have the same opportunities you hadI am not asking for more than that.Give our young people the same opportunities you had when you were their age.Then, you will have a clear conscience, you will know you have done your best by giving them the programs they need to have the same opportunities you had.Supply.

subsubtopic: FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT RELATING TO CONSTRUCTION AND EQUIPMENT OF SCHOOLS-QUEBEC
clean_text: Is there a financial assistance agreement between the government and the Government of the Province of Quebec concerning the construction and equipment of schools and, if so (a) what is its nature (b) on what date was it signed (c) for what school level

subsubtopic: Opposition Motion--Federal Excise Tax on Gasoline
clean_text: Mr.Speaker, I am pleased to speak to the motion by the Liberal Party.The purpose of the motion is essentially to make what has been called the gas tax rebate to municipalities permanent. Ultimately, we all know that this is all about appearances.The gas tax is not going directly to the cities.Only part of it is.That is the essence of what the Liberals introduced as part of their effort to tackle the infrastructure deficit.That fight has become very important here in Parliament, because it is ultimately here that the problem being experienced in all cities in Canada, including in Quebec, in terms of that infrastructure deficit begins. Why does it begin hereThe Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister of Finance started out well when he was explaining to us just now that Trudeau and the Liberals, followed by the Conservatives, had left us a deficit of over $120 billion.The parliamentary secretary concluded by telling us that the debt had been reduced to $12 billion.It seems to me that the figure he was reading from his BlackBerry was not right, because the last year the Conservatives were in power, the debt grew by $12 billion. Today, I can understand that the Conservatives are uncomfortable with the debt, a large part of which was left to us by them.In fact we have the Conservatives to thank for the bulk of the present debt.They would very much like to eliminate the debt with the surpluses they are producing, surpluses that the Bloc Québécois has estimated at $69 billion over the next five years. The problem, in terms of the Conservatives’ terrible management methods, is that in the meantime there is a whole domino effect.Under the Chrétien government, with the member for  Lasalle—Émard as Minister of Finance, the Liberals decided to cut transfers to the provinces in order to achieve a zero deficit, because in order to eliminate the debt they had to start by stopping spending.That is the reality and no one can deny it.They cut health and education transfers. The result for all the provinces was terrible.Our Liberal colleague gave the example of Ontario earlier.The same thing was done in Quebec.To try to maintain the same health care and education services, the decision was made to have the other levels of government pay a share, even though they are not recognized as governments.Municipalities and school boards can tax, but they are not recognized as governments, even in Quebec.And there was pressure brought to bear on those levels to increase their contribution..Those who are somewhat familiar with the political history of the 1990s in Quebec will recall that the first reform was called the Ryan reform, under a Liberal government.The decision was made to transfer responsibility for a majority of roadworks and roads that were not socalled “national” roads to the cities.Across Quebec, municipalities found themselves with 4,000 bridges, overpasses, real works of art, culverts, and other road infrastructures, but were given no money to maintain them..It was simple: the government had no money, so the cities had to be capable of maintaining them.The result was that no maintenance was done.That situation has reached the point that two months ago the government of Quebec decided to take back responsibility for the 4,000 overpasses, saying that the cities had not been up to it.Quebec is therefore going to have to pay to rebuild those infrastructures itself..If the cities were unable to maintain the infrastructures for which the Government of Quebec had given them responsibility, it was the same thing for their own infrastructures because the transfer of responsibility to the cities included not only roads but also services. More and more, cities have been forced to take charge of other things besides services.In theory, property taxes are supposed to be reserved for services to buildings.It is probably the most regressive tax imposed on our fellow citizens by cities.It is a property tax; it is not a service to the public.It should not be used to underwrite a series of programs introduced by the cities or transferred directly to them.They have increased the burden of services to the public delivered by the cities.Probably, this has been done in all Canadian provinces.At any rate, it has been done in Quebec.All of that came about because the federal government decided to achieve a zero deficit.Since then, it has begun to accumulate surpluses. The Liberals have explained that they tried to introduce support programs, probably because they felt embarrassed.Today, after 13 years of Liberal rule, the Conservatives are even more embarrassed because they are the ones who left behind the bulk of the debt and the problems associated with reducing or eliminating the debt.They are trying to create programs, but the malaise is real and very widespread..Today's remarks by the Minister of Finance illustrate this; so do those of the Minister of Transport, Infrastructure and Communitiess.Last month, the Canadian Federation of Municipalities released a report which showed that the current infrastructure deficit is $123 billion. The amount of money that cities would need in 2007 to bring their infrastructures up to standard is $123 billion.We are not talking about rebuilding but just about maintenance to bring everything up to a safe and secure level for the public. The Conservative government is proposing to inject $33 billion over a period of seven years.That amount does not apply to this year.Often, they throw out the figure of $33 billion, so that, once again, the public is misled.People might feel that it is a good thing the government is putting up $33 billion to meet the deficit of $123 billion.But no, that amount is not being injected this year.It is spread over seven years.What is more, not all of the $33 billion will go to municipal infrastructure.The public should not be mislead either..Unless I am mistaken, we are talking about $33 billion that will indeed be injected into infrastructure.However, the amount going to cities is less than $33 billion.In particular, the building Canada fund involves $8. billion.There are negotiations with the provinces; and they may add other projects, perhaps airports or many other kinds of infrastructures that will require lots of money but which are not directly related to the needs of the cities..It is important to understand that.It is the position that the Bloc Québécois has traditionally taken: the infrastructure deficit must be resolved.The federal government must understand that the reason why the cities have a $123 billion infrastructure deficit is that it slashed its transfers to the provinces, which then downloaded on the cities, with the result that they can no longer afford to maintain their infrastructure properly.That is the reality..Where does this amount come fromIt is important to know exactly.The $123 billion comes from a scientific study—the only scientific study that the Federation of Canadian Municipalities has done.That is why everyone was surprised.It was not expected because of how hard it is to do a study of this kind when there are more than 4,000 cities and towns all across Canada.So the study was done.It was the first time that someone had asked every city to fill out a form detailing its needs.Then expert analysts and engineers studied the whole situation and arrived at a total of $123 billion..What does this meanIt means $31 billion for water supply systems and sewage, $21. billion for transportation, and $22. billion for public transit.People know what the problems are with public transit.In Quebec, there are problems with the metro in Montreal.Very large sums will have to be invested.Some metro lines simply stop because the infrastructure itself is crumbling in places.Cracks are appearing and pieces of cement fall off.That is the reality.There are also $40. billion for cultural and social infrastructure and $7. billion for waste management..All this is ultimately due to the way in which the federal government decided to restore its own financial health by squeezing the provinces..All parties in the House must acknowledge a certain reality: cities are the creatures of the provinces.Any attempt to deal directly with cities is therefore contrary to the Canadian constitution adopted by most of the parties here.Any direct negotiations or direct agreements between cities and the federal government would be contrary to the proper procedure..The Bloc Québécois has always maintained that we must deal with this $123 billion deficit.In order to do so, there must be direct transfers from the Government of Canada to the government of each of the provinces.There needs to be a single transfer so that the provinces—Quebec, Ontario and the others—can establish infrastructure programs.What is attractive about this is that when the provinces set out to do it, they often make some investments of their own.As a result, the $123 billion can probably be divided into three parts: the federal government’s share, the share of each of the provinces, and the cities’ share so that the infrastructure deficit can finally be eliminated..hether it is the Liberal Party deciding to create one, two or three infrastructure programs or the Conservative Party adding programs, in the end, under the Canadian Constitution, that party has no choice about negotiating with each province..he building Canada fund has been mentioned; it is the latest program announced in the 2007 budget.Quebec has not yet received one penny of this fund, because the agreement with the Province of Quebec has not yet been signed.In fact, to score political points, the government tried to please everyone by creating a framework within which offers are made to each city, whereas in fact, according to the Constitution, cities have to negotiate with the provinces.The whole process therefore becomes bogged down in discussions and negotiations.The federal government is trying to have the right to interfere, especially the Conservatives; it was less flagrant when the Liberals were in power, because they had a good grasp of the Canadian Constitution.The Conservatives, likely because they want to have a majority at any cost, are using every means possible to try to direct policies and impose their conditions on the provinces.For that reason, Quebec still has not reached an agreement on the building Canada fund.The $8. billion agreement has not been signed.It is all well and good to tell us today that the infrastructure budget is $33 billion.It is true that the program that uses the excise tax on gasoline is already established.The Conservatives cannot do anything about this, because the Liberals introduced this program.The funding amounts are known; cities know that until 2012, they will be receiving their share of that tax.What I take exception to is that today the Liberals want to extend this established program by eliminating the 2012 end date and making the program permanent.In fact, this is not what cities need.The $123 billion infrastructure deficit must really be corrected, and this will take more than simply negotiating with each province.Moreover, they have to stop acting like the Conservatives who are trying to create new programs and, more importantly, give themselves the power to make choices on behalf of the cities and provinces, although cities are creatures of the provinces.For the Bloc Québécois, it is simple: had the decision been made, Quebec would be a country today and we would have resolved the matter a long time ago by negotiating directly with our cities.However, there is the Canadian Constitution.I am always taken aback when the federalist parties do not respect it and that happens every time we talk about infrastructure.Why do they do thatIt comes down to electioneering.Once again, with the building Canada fund, they are attempting to create various funds to be distributed to the cities by the office of the Minister of Transport, Infrastructure and Communities or that of the Minister of the Economic Development Agency of Canada for the Regions of Quebec, giving them the impression that it is a gift.If there were some awareness of the problems created by attempting to balance the budget by downloading responsibilities onto the provinces—which in turn balanced their health and education budgets at the expense of cities—the issue of the $123 billion would be resolved.There would finally be money for everyone.There would be only one program and all cities could rest assured that, by the end of the negotiations, they could deal with their infrastructure deficit.Thus, cities would not have to fight one another to see which one would be first or second.The program should be announced, specifying that it would extend over five years, for example, giving each city that time to work things out and solve their infrastructure problems by the end of that period.The cities could arrange their loans and negotiate accordingly.The necessary money is not always available for the cities' share of funding; loans must be arranged and the citizens convinced.In short, if there were only one program, things would be much simpler.Unfortunately, the government in Ottawa is once again bypassing the Constitution for purely partisan reasons.It has decided to go over Quebec's head and is trying to negotiate directly with the cities.As a result, no agreement on the building Canada fund has been signed in Quebec.Only two provinces—British Columbia and Nova Scotia, if I am not mistaken—have signed agreements so far, even though this fund was announced in budget 2007.Once again, it is easy to understand.The Bloc Québécois is all in favour of dealing with the cities' infrastructure deficit.We want this $123 billion deficit of the cities in Quebec and the rest of Canada to be paid off once and for all.We would like this to be, to be applied to solving infrastructure problems.That way, each province could negotiate with its cities as to when each city could receive their share.That would be a very easy and realistic approach that is respectful of the fact that the cities, in large part, had to pay down the federal government's deficit.It was the Liberals and the Conservatives who added to the deficit.They flagrantly forget history.Perhaps some—because they are new—do not remember.Nonetheless, the cities are running deficits for their infrastructure today because in the 1990s, the Liberal government had to wipe out the deficit that had been shamelessly created by the Conservatives and the Trudeau government.The Conservative member mentioned it earlier, and he was right.To try to pay off the annual deficit, to try to pay its grocery bill, the government had to cut transfers to the various provinces for health and education.It was easy to do.The federal government does not provide services in health and education.Why not cut transfers to the provinces and let them sort it outThe provinces cannot perform miracles to maintain the same level of health and education services..ven Jean Chrétien had the nasty habit of saying that although he was cutting transfer payments, he was the one setting the national standards.Imagine how this nice, beautiful Canada was built.He was not even the one providing the service, but he set the national standard in order to get reelected.And people bought that line and said that he was defending their interests.But that is not at all what happened.He was cutting transfer payments to the provinces, who in turn had to cut services or try to find money elsewhere.What did they do in most casesThey transferred responsibility to the municipalities, who had no money themselves.The municipalities ended up fixing the interior of the house, but not the foundation or the structure.All municipal infrastructures suffered.I am very proud of the study done by the Federation of Canadian Municipalities, which I have examined.It is the first serious study.The $123 billion infrastructure deficit is accurate.It is the first time a researcher has really examined this issue.The 4,000 municipalities were asked to describe their own problems with infrastructure deficits.We must start addressing this now.We must not do what the Liberals did and keep saying we will fix this by extending the gas tax rebate.Besides, what government would dare eliminate the gas tax transfer in five yearsIt is already guaranteed until 2012.I do not see how the government could get rid of gas tax transfers to municipalities.It would pay the political price..he question today is not about making the gas tax rebate permanent.There is a much more serious deficit.The gas tax rebate represents $11. billion over five years.And this year's needs, if we wanted to wipe out the infrastructure deficit for all municipalities, total $123 billion.Thus, it is clear that this $11. billion over five years is—yes, it is true—part of a solution.However, at present, we are not about to try to do what the Liberals did, by claiming that this is the one and only solution, that we came up with it, and that this is what municipalities need.They need more than that.This is what the Conservative government should focus on.Furthermore, the Bloc Québécois is trying to exert pressure where pressure is needed, so that all the federalist parties in this House clearly understand that this country, Canada, has a Constitution, which specifies that cities and towns are under provincial jurisdiction.These federalist parties must understand that, if they want to negotiate, there must be a direct agreement.If they are going to tackle the $123 billion, they must negotiate.We must ensure that all levels of government participate and that the federal government will make a transfer payment to Quebec.The province, in cooperation with its cities and towns, can then distribute annually the amounts needed for infrastructures.We hope this can all be spread over five years, so that each of the towns and cities can really resolve its infrastructure deficit problem by the end of that period.That would be excellent.However, once again, the Conservatives will not have the courage, because they will likely try to fix one mistake with another.They will try to pay off Canada's accumulated debt and forget that, in the meantime, cities and towns are going further into debt for their infrastructures.I am not saying that they will borrow the money, necessarily, because some of them do not even have the financial ability to do so.Instead, they will continue to allow infrastructures to deteriorate.One day, citizens will pay the price.Once again, I hope it will not be the citizens of municipalities, when the problem lies here, in Ottawa.

subtopic: ANNUAL FINANCIAL STATEMENT OF THE MINISTER OF FINANCE
clean_text: Mr.Speaker, after hearing the speech of the Minister of Finance (Mr.Abbott) on February 19 last and after having studied it thoroughly, I deemed it my duty to visit my constituency in order to find out the opinion of the electorate and their appreciation of the budget.I might say that the workers and the farmers of my constituency have reacted to the budget in the most favourable and satisfactory manner, even though they expected a more substantial cut.The ideal would probably have been to obtain straight exemptions, but the international situation must be taken into consideration, as well as the numerous pieces of social legislation which the Liberal government has granted to the population of our country.A glance at the statements appearing in the foreign press show that the feat performed by the Minister of Finance is considered as truly stupendous, not to say miraculous, whatever the member for Labelle (Mr.Courtemanche) may say to the contrary.One thing is sure, it is that the last budget reached all classes of society and resulted in a general tax reduction.I shall not undertake to list the numerous exemptions which the Canadian people enjoy.It is sufficient for me to say that the tax rates have returned to the normal levels of 1949 and that in the last two budgets the Minister of Finance has reduced taxes by over $513 million.Obviously, this is frowned upon by the opposition groups whose criticisms are rank contradictions when the various statements are considered.Some claim it is a preelection budget which favours the low income groups, that is the majority of taxpayers.That was the first reaction of the hon.member for Greenwood (Mr.Macdonnell); others contend that it is a rich man's budget; others still say that it is a budget which favours corporations.I wish to discuss the matter in order to prove that no one was overlooked in this budget.For this purpose, may I quote statistics in connection with my own constituency in order to show the benefits provided by the budget.I have here a newspaper which reported last Thursday that in my district alone, that is to say in La Mauricie, more than 4,000 tons of newsprint were shipped every day over one railway line.As that newsprint.The BudgetMr.Rochefort sells for $125 a ton, we may say that the value of such production exceeds $500,000 a day.In my home constituency, at Cap de la Madeleine and Three Rivers, 2,500 tons of newsprint are produced every day, which represents a value of more than $300,000, and the 2,500 men who find employment in that industry draw more than $100,000 a day in wages.At Cap de la Madeleine, in 1950, out of 5,941 wage earners, there were only 2,300 taxpayers.That is to say that with 3,641 people paying no tax, 60 per cent of the workers, men and women, in that town were not taxable and, in fact, did not pay tax.The 2,300 taxpayers paid $320,000 on a reported income of $6,436,000, in other words $1 for each $20 earned.Since then the government removed the 20 per cent surtax for national defence; also the workers had their wages increased in 1952 by 12 per cent.I have here a newspaper excerpt, announcing an increase of 10 per cent for employees of the paper mills in my constituency, starting March 1, 1953 and of 6 cents an hour retroactive to May 1952.To this must be added the latest tax reduction of 11 per cent, effective July 1, 1953.The paper mill workers of my constituency earn between $60 and $150 a week.Once they have deducted their personal and their dependents' allowances, contributions to charitable organizations, medical or hospital expenses, most of them, that is approximately 60 per cent, pay no income tax whatever, the remainder paying a moderate amount, which has constantly been decreasing with every budget brought down lately.Moreover the government having managed to check inflation, the cost of living has gone down 2 per cent.This decrease has brought, about a general fall in food and clothing prices.The same obtains in every industry in my constituency where the salary scale is everywhere 300 or 400 per cent higher than when the Tory party administered the affairs of this country during the 193035 period.Compare the salaries earned today with those we have known during the hunger and misery stricken, debtridden period between 1930 and 1935, when this country was governed by the Conservative party.I met this week a worker who supervises a paper machine; he earned $48 a month in 1932, because his mill was operating only two days a week; now, under a Liberal regime, this same worker earns $860 a month..he man earns $4,000 a year more than the member for that county.Of course, he pays income tax, but he is glad to do so, because he has the salary required to pay it, while he was starving under the Conservatives.Today this worker has a bank account which increases every day, he owns his own house, his children are attending schools, he has a son at the university, he even has his own car; he knows now what wellbeing and comfort are, what it is to be welloff, while in the former days, under a Conservative regime, he knew but misery, starvation and debts.Ask him, even if he now pays income tax, whether he would change his present status with that of the years 19301935.It is the same with all workers of the county of Champlain and, mind you, the salary scales have more than doubled in the course of the last few years in all these industries.And yet 60 per cent of all these workers pay no tax.How can you reduce the tax of the lower income bracket when it pays no tax at allhe minimum salary paid since March 1 last in paper mills is $1.8 per hour and the maximum salary, $2.5 per hour, which amounts to nearly $24 a day.Working conditions are excellent.This worker enjoys three weeks' holidays with pay.He contributes to a pension fund.In case of illness in hospital he and the members of his family are assured of special assistance.In the plant he is given free medical and nursing care.Go and ask this worker if he is willing to exchange the system of the present Liberal government for the system of direct relief tokens, of Murling shelters, of soup kitchens, an institution greatly favoured by the Conservative party when it was in power from 1930 to 1935.In every one of the factories in my constituency, I would find the same working conditions, the same wages and the same good employeremployee relations.In my riding there are three newsprint mills, three paper bag factories, one enormous paper foils factory, which plans to expand shortly, two abrasive manufacturing plants, about ten textile plants, a shoe factory, six glove factories, smelters and over forty private undertakings.All those plants are operating at full capacity.Salaries range from a minimum of 90 cents per hour to $2.Here is real prosperity for which the Liberal government of this country is responsible.Its achievements have nothing to do with.petty quarrels arising from political patronage; its policy is not to enrich its friends or to fill the pork barrel.At the beginning of the session, the Prime Minister (Mr.St.Laurent) pointed out to the house that if the suggestions of the Leader of the Opposition and his lieutenants were to be implemented, taxes would double in this country.After listing the latest requests of the Conservative party, the Prime Minister said that it would be necessary to increase if not to double taxes in order to meet all the requests of the opposition.Moreover, he stated that the result would be a deficit of half a billion dollars a year.The few members that make up the Conservative opposition from Quebec, with the help of the independents, consider themselves without any leader and without a policy.They try to deal with provincial matters and come into this house to make speeches which we have read almost word for word in newspapers and which have already been heard in a legislature, which is not the House of Commons.Did the electors of these counties give them a federal or a provincial mandateTheir electors may well ask them if they have not diverted the federal mandate they have been entrusted with to serve their own purposesI will simply ask them the following question: If the party they support were in power, would they try to take away the revenue of the country and turn it over to the provincese have reason to question the good faith of their arguments and to consider their statements rather as a joke.We can ask ourselves whether these false autonomists are not, rather, seeking electoral funds to ensure their reelection in their own constituencies.Are these people really trying in this house to promote the interests of Quebec; we have every reason to doubt it.The interests of our country and our province in particular are more secure in the hands of the present Prime Minister than in the hands of those people.The interests of this country are better protected by the Liberal party than by any other party.The fairness of the social legislation of the Liberal government proves it.Let them show me in what way Quebec has been unfairly treated.Let them point out in what way the treatment meted out to our people was inferior to the treatment of the other provinces.Should not the central power continue to retain its own constitutional sources of revenue, and that in order to make further progress towards the implementation of its social legislation program,.8108m.he BudgetMr.Rochefort towards its program of defence against foreign enemies, and more particularly in order to grant equal treatment to all the provinces as far as the federal field is concerned.Beware of those false prophets who thus try to hide the little influence they exert in this house and on the electors of this country.The opposition has its uses, it must exist, but only in so far as it concerns itself with its own responsibilities, as it remains in its allotted field, in this case the federal field.It must not move away from that field in the least; that is the mandate entrusted to it.In order to prove the sincerity of the Liberal party and of its present leader, here are two statements made by the Prime Minister.The first dates back to August 7, 1948:.anada must be a political union of two great races, prepared by men of vision and tolerance, a union in which the two associates would preserve their essential character, their relationship, their language and their culture.Is there any better or more sincere evidence than this, the statement of this country's leaderHere is another statement made by the Prime Minister, on October 28, 1952:.o my mind the Liberal party's aim must be to maintain this country's unity and security, as the home of a free people.The government must then, having ensured national security, concern itself mainly with the freedom and welfare of the individual and of the family.Can there be greater evidence of faith, honour and honesty, of faithfulness to his own kindred than these words uttered by the Liberal leader who is at the head of government at the present timehe opposition asks for income tax reductions and at the same time requests pensions and subsidies which would inevitably increase these taxes.The result would be a deficit ranging from half a billion to one billion a year if these recommendations were implemented.Let us now see what goes on on the Liberal side.The national debt has been reduced by $2J billion since 1946.Here is what can really be called sound administration.Here is a truly stable policy applied by a stable government.The Conservative party will say for its part that it is a companies' budget; they said the contrary last year.To this must be added what everyone will admit: it is the companies which support our population and provide the means of living of nearly all our workers by paying them a salary.If the plants were.816	HOUSE OF.he BudgetMr.Rochejort shut down our workers would be reduced to destitution.The moment had come to help industry and the Liberal government saw to it in the last budget.These new exemptions will allow companies to keep on growing and developing, to extend their facilities, and thus be in a position to employ a greater number of people.There is not a single field in which the present Liberal government do not pursue their aim, which is to help the Canadian people.A government which pays out $348 million a year in family allowances, nearly $2 million a year in my county, that is what the Progressive Conservative party calls waste and extravagance.A government which pays out $344 million in old age pensions, and nearly $2 million a year in my county alone, is that what the Progressive Conservative party calls waste and extravaganceA government which pays out $48 million in public health grants, including $30 million a year for hospital constructionmy county alone has received a cash grant of $139,500 for the Cloutier hospital, at Cap de la Madeleine is that what the Conservative party calls government waste and extravagancen this connection, I may say that the Hon.the Minister of National Health and Welfare (Mr.Martin) sent me this week a letter telling me that the first grant was duly paid on December 4, 1951, and that it was handed to the nuns 5 months later.The balance, which amounted to $34,875 was sent by the department on October 20, 1952; last Sunday evening, the nuns told me that the balance of the grant had not yet reached them.But the Liberal government goes further still.It pays its share of the old age pension for persons between 65 and 70 years of age.The present government pays its share of the pension for the blind.All classes of society profit by the social measures of our present Liberal government.The disabled people remain to be helped and in that field the government will soon take the necessary steps.This is what we call legislating for the people.In other fields, the government is ready to provide assistance, particularly to our universities which are in a critical position because the provincial government has refused to accept this assistance.Just recently, I was talking to a professor in a university who had been drawing a salary of $800 a year after twenty years of teaching; last year his salary was increased to $3,300 as a result of the federal grants.Unfortunately, this year he was informed that he would revert to his old salary..For the first time, thanks to federal aid, our university professors received, last year, a fair salary.We should not be surprised to see desertions occur among faculty members following the refusal of the provincial government to accept this assistance and the entire responsibility for the setback suffered by those universities will rest upon the government of Quebec.How is it then that grants towards the building of technical schools, schools of arts and crafts, schools where papermaking is taught, all in the field of education, are acceptedThese grants are found to be convenient since they can be used for political patronage through the letting of contracts, without tenders, for the building of schools.An arts and crafts school is now being built in my riding, at Cap de la Madeleine, for which the federal government is paying more than $275,000.In a public statement, the provincial member said that for two years he had been looking forward to the implementation of that project.However, during the same period, he had entirely forgotten to ask for tenders.The pinpose of this was to favour one of his friends with this contract, just after the provincial elections.The government of this country pays its share of the scholarships granted to the students of our universities and our technical schools.Yet, this comes under the heading of education.Those grants are accepted and then used in certain cases for political patronage.The government also spends huge amounts of money to assist our veterans.It also contributes to the progress of the Central Housing and Mortgage Corporation, which has led to the construction of 300 new dwellings at Cap de la Madeleine.The Liberal government is encouraging cooperatives which are developing rapidly.However, it should look into the possibility of reducing a little the down payment, so as to enable the working people to purchase a house for their family.Public works also help people in our counties, more particularly through the construction of retaining walls.The Department of Transport operates in my constituency a railway yard that provides employment for more than 200 people at Garneau.I would ask the Minister of Transport (Mr.Chevrier) to make even greater use of that central point, where the Canadian National and the Canadian Pacific railway meet; all traffic from Abitibi, Lake St.John, Montreal and Quebec pass through there.In my opinion, the Post Office Department provides an almost perfect service; however, I would ask the Hon.the Postmaster General (Mr.Cote) to reestablish, if possible, the.twiceaday delivery system.The people of our towns would deeply appreciate a return to this service.The assistance of the Liberal government in Ottawa is not limited to towns and workers alone, but also extends to farmers.The government, under its price support legislation, gives assistance to the country as a whole and, particularly, to the agricultural class.We have to express our appreciation to the Hon.the Minister of Agriculture (Mr.Gardiner) whose foresight prevented a shortage of butter this winter and an increase in the price of this product.The Liberal government in Ottawa helps the farmers by subsidizing the transportation of grain and feed to the eastern part of the country.The government also pays subsidies on bacon hogs.Through its Cold Storage Act the Department of Agriculture assists the dairy industry by paying 50 per cent of the cost of constructing and equipping buildings whenever there is an amalgamation of cheese factories.It also helps through subsidization of fairs.In this connection, I would ask the government to amend the present legislation in order to assist class B fairs which are practically without revenue and which need aid far more than class A fairs.Those are various ways in which the federal government assists agriculture.And then again we have the intimate relationship between the Department of Trade and Commerce and the other departments.This department is the one which obtains foreign outlets for our farmers, our industrialists or our businessmen, by means of agreements and treaties made with foreign countries.There is no section of our people that does not benefit from the policies of the present government, of its leader (Mr.St.Laurent), of the members of his cabinet and of all the Liberal members of this house.The Unemployment Insurance Act is a piece of Liberal legislation.Every day the unemployed individual, for as long as he is out of work, is entitled to benefits under its provisions.The scope of this legislation has been considerably extended, since all occupations are now insurable.The allowances have been increased.The waiting period has been reduced.The seasonal worker has not been ignored by the Liberal government of the present Prime Minister.One of his first moves when he took power in 1949 was to establish supplementary allowances which vere gladly received by the lumbering vorkcrs of my county.^ I should like to refer for a moment, Mr.Speaker, to the great number of workers of ny county who spend the winter months in.8108I79i.he BudgetMr.Rochefort lumbering camps.From 1930 to 1935, when the country was under a Conservative regime, the lumbermen of my county earned $18, $20 or $25 a month.Food left much to be desired.They were badly sheltered.Working conditions were difficult.Today these same lumbermen earn from $15 to $30 a day.That is, they get $7.0 a cord and many of them cut up to four cords a day, thanks to modern equipment.Ask the people in my constituency if they want a change of government.They are not old enough yet to have forgotten what they went through from 1930 to 1935, when the country suffered a setback on all fronts.The young people in my riding cannot readily forget the benefits they have received from our government.The old people of my riding and of the whole country will ever be grateful to the Prime Minister of this country for having given them the Old Age Security Act.That was one of the first steps taken by our present Prime Minister.The working people of my constituency and of the whole country will long remember the Liberal government which keeps foreign and domestic trade at such high levels and allows the worker to earn wages comparable only to those paid in the United States.The farmers also have their share of this prosperity.The recent trip of the Right Hon.the Minister of Trade and Commerce (Mr.Howe) to South America has opened unlimited vistas to our farmers and manufacturers, and we have already felt its good effects.I do not contend, however, that everything is perfect.The international situation is still strained.Do you believe that a new government would abolish taxesWhere is the constructive program of the Conservative partyCriticism has been directed towards the Department of National Defence and the Department of Defence Production.Let us not forget, in the first place, that hundreds of millions of dollars are paid in salaries to our Canadian workers as a result of this program The Liberal government has fortunately outlined a defence policy to protect our country within and without; well then, should our country be the victim of a surprise attack which would catch us unprepared, I can see the members of the Conservative party cry out that the Liberal government has not been extravagant but improvident.The government does not resort to any false pretences; its record is one of which we may well be proud.Let us not pay any attention to those troublemakers who do not know.818	HOUSE OF.he BudgetMr.Hodgson where to turn in order to save the Tory party which is on its last legs.These very people who made so much fuss about the Currie report did not utter a word when faced with the author of the report in the committee on defence expenditure.The Liberal government, which represents everywhere and in every province the Canadian spirit, is preserving peace.We must be ready to meet any emergencies and must not let the enemy gain a foothold in our country, as it has done in certain European countries.We are masters here and we intend to remain so forever.The Liberal party is the one that offers the greatest guarantees in this connection.It is the only party that preaches national unity in our country and that is truly representative of that unity.The Canadian people are happy and live happily.The bank savings of our working people continue to increase, which is a sure sign of prosperity, and that prosperity, far from decreasing, is increasing each year.That is why the Liberal government deserves our confidence.The beautiful county of Champlain and the country at large will, for a long time yet, place their confidence in that government when the time comes to make a decision.(Text):.

subtopic: Protection of Communities and Exploited Persons Act
clean_text: Mr.Speaker, I would like to thank the parliamentary secretary for his question and comment.Perhaps he is right.I may feel that the wording is inappropriate, but perhaps it is used elsewhere.I would like to thank him for pointing that out.In any event, I would like to continue with what I saying.I still find it odd.Of course, I do not want to see prostitutes next to a schoolyard.The hon.member used that example earlier, in his speech.There is a high school near my house, and I too would be concerned to see prostitutes or pimps there recruiting young people who are at the school..his bill does not provide any tools and does not even attempt to determine why prostitution exists.I think that the main focus of our work here is to figure out what we can do so that prostitution no longer exists.We can regulate it and put all kinds of provisions in place, but we need to ask the fundamental question of what needs to be done so that there are fewer prostitutes in our country.We can criminalize them as much as we want, but that will not reduce the number of sex workers.That is what I think we should be focusing on, together.Many national groups in Canada would be willing to work with us to reach that goal.That is the direction we should be heading in.

subsubtopic: REASON FOR DELAY IN CONSULTING PROVINCES ON PROGRAMS TO CREATE EMPLOYMENT
clean_text: Mr.Speaker, I would like to direct a question to the Minister of Manpower and Immigration, who I know is interested in unemployment, or at least in his employment strategy.Given the fact that the University of Toronto Institute for Policy Analysis and the Conference Board of Canada have indicated that in terms of 1977 and years following we are going to experience very.November 9, 1976.ral Questions.luggish growth and higher unemployment, which is, of course, little encouragement to those who are presently unemployed or those who will be unemployed, why is it necessary to wait until January 1977 to commence discussions with the provinces on a consultative basis regarding unemployment programs, as envisaged by the employment strategy, and has the minister actually stated that he finds it impossible to implement these programs before April, 1977

I shall only mention a few of them.First, there is the Paris agency, $6,500, a new service entirely.You cannot make an omelet without breaking some eggs.Look at the magnificent trade omelet we have made for this country.One of the eggs that we have broken to get this trade omelet is the Paris agency, which has cost us $6,500, a mere bagatelle compared with the increased commerce we will get from that source in the future.The next is the astronomical observatory and apparatus, $10,497.5.That is a service in the interest of science.The next is the result of the increase in the gold output consequent upon the discovery of gold in the Yukon, being the assay office, tlie increase being $14,993.0.Hon.gentlemen opposite did not require anything of the sort ; in fact, I do not know that in those days there was really enough gold in Canada to require an assay to be made of it.Then, we return to Messers.Petersen, Tait & Company, the amount of their deposit, $56,940.That was money which they had paid into the treasury.They got it back out of the treasury and there was no loss in the transaction.The next is halfbreed claims commission, $2,978.4.Why do I mention such a small sum as this Just to emphasize the businesslike methods of this government in contradistinction to the unbusinesslike methods of the late government.When these.halfbreeds made claims to the present government their claims were submitted to a commission and settled, with the result that these halfbreeds are loyal and contented citizens of the Dominion.In the old days these halfbreeds had claims, they sent these claims to the Dominion government, the Dominion government pigeonholed them or threw them into the wastepaper basket and the result was that we had a serious rebellion in the Northwest Territories which cost between $5,000,000 and $6,000,000, to say nothing of the scores of bright young lives which were given up in the service of the country on that occasion.There is something in the association of the ideas and the association of ideas is so strong in this case that I must be pardoned for having brought this item to the attention of the House.The next item is engraving maps of the Dominion and Northwest Territories, $14,096.3.We know how this has increased the knowledge of the Dominion both within the confines of Canada and beyond them.It is part of that advertising which is bringing such rich results in men and money.Hon.gentlemen will not complain of that.Department of Labourand now I come to some more of that charming campaign literature$20,471.4.Will hon.gentlemen say they do not want the Department of Labour Will they say that if they came into power they would abolish the Department of Labour I think not.They dare not do it, however much they might wish to and I do not say they wish to.I have here pamphlet No.8, headed ' Starvation Wages. Then follows a schedule of the wages paid on the Intercolonial Railway that hon.gentlemen opposite say are not enough, and that the government think also are not enough, for since the time that those wages were paid they have increased them.This is another example of the hypocrisy of hon.gentlemen opposite in claiming that the expenditure is too great because here is an item in regard to which they want to make it still more.I do not complain so much of that as I do of this which is only second to that pamphlet from which I quoted a short time ago headed ' .unketing Ministers. In this pamphlet, No.8, these hon.gentlemen say :.nd this is the government that says the workingmen should be grateful to it because it is paying a ' minister of labour ' $8,000 a year !.on.gentlemen opposite very well know the miserable insinuation contained in that clause and the absolute falsity that lies within the four corners of that half truth.They surely know that the Minister of Labour is also the Postmaster General, that ns Postmaster General he draws exactly the salary which his Conservative predecessor, as Postmaster General drew, and that in addition to the salary of the Postmaster Mr.A.T.THOMPSON.General he does not draw one single cent of pay for the immensely increased amount of labour which he in his generous anxiety for the good of the labouring classes has taken upon his shoulders.Marconi wireless, $10,000.We led in assisting that young man to whom the great universities of the old world are now stretching forth a hand and upon whom they are showering honours and conferring degrees.We took him in when he was more friendless than he is now and gave him financial aid.His method has proved to be immensely valuable beyond peradventure, and we in Canada have the honour of having been in the forefront in assisting in the development of this modern wonder of science from which we will reap substantial financial benefits far beyond our investment.We have contributed $60,000 toward the Ottawa Improvement fund.Who will cavil at that 1 Not hon.gentlemen opposite.Sir, that vote of the money of the people of Canada for the improvement of Canada's capital is an outward and visible proof that Canada is no longer a number of isolated provinces experimentally strung together by a British Act of parliament, but that Canada is a united nation from ocean to ocean and from the southern boundary to the Artie sea, one and indivisable.Who will abject to the beautifying of our capital as the capitals of all other countries are beautified at the general expense of the people For the consolidation of the statutes we spent $12,722.8.Who will say, especially those in the legal profession, that that expenditure has been made before its time Alaskan boundary commission, $59,993.That is an expenditureI may perhaps be pardoned for saying iton a necessary evil.Hon.gentlemen will be surprised to learn that also in this delightful literature which has furnished us with so much instruction and amusement, the visit of the hon.Minister of the Interior to England last summer, where he worked, as, I suppose few men have ever worked in the interests of Canada, is actually put under the heading * junketing tours. What do you think of that, Mr.Speaker A junketing tour.That important mission of the.on.the Minister of the Interior classed as one of the junketing tours ! These sums taken together reach a total of $269,193.4 and by tracing out other and smaller items I could very easily account for the small balance of $60,000.but I shall not attempt to do so as I do
