immigrants, but rather against the Department of Citizenship and Immigration, which does not take into consideration the economic conditions prevailing in this country, when the government intends to receive 100,000 immigrants per year.Mr.Chairman, may I refer to my own area as an example.Immigrants arrive with employment contracts in their pockets, which enable them to get a job immediately in one of the mines: Noranda, Bourlamaque, Sigma or Malartic.Try to imagine the reaction of Canadians who have lived in this area for 10, 15 or 20 years and have had to queue up at the unemployment insurance office for a.year or two, because a few mines have closed down.As I said, those Canadian unemployed have nothing against the immigrants, but they have resentment against the Department of Citizenship and Immigration, which nevertheless authorizes 100,000 immigrants per year to enter this country when they cannot find jobs in the same industries.Mr.Chairman, while 100,000 immigrants are coming in annually from different countries of the world, we have this paradoxical situation that, in the meantime, thousands of Canadians cross the border to the south to settle down in the United States.Surely there are reasons which prompt our Canadian fellow citizens to do so, especially at a time when the department is encouraging the immigration of 100,000 persons a year.Why do Canadians emigrate to the United StatesSurely there are basic economical reasons.Our people probably get better salaries in the United States, better employment opportunities, and so they leave Canada, their parish, their province and their friends to settle down in a foreign country.Mr.Chairman, I think the Department of Citizenship and Immigration should take those facts into consideration.I completely agree with the hon.member for Fraser Valley (Mr.Patterson) when he says that it would be possible to bring in more immigrants than we are doing, but not at a time when we cannot even guarantee employment and security for our own citizens.If we had an economic system providing for the economic development of Canada, then, I think it would be easy to increase materially the number of immigrants coming to Canada, because I submit everything has yet to be done in our country, we have hardly begun developing its physical possibilities.If our financial and monetary situation allowed us to begin those developments, security and freedom would be guaranteed to all our citizens within two years, and that, even if we were bringing in more immigrants than we are doing at the present time because then it would be sensible for them to come and live in a new country where everything remains to be done, where natural resources are practically undeveloped.Mr.Chairman, as far as immigration is concerned, I call the minister's attention on this point: there are Canadians who are not satisfied and unfortunately, some of them blame the immigrants because they think they are taking their jobs.I do not think so.But it is obvious that we will have to try and solve that problem, about which our Canadian citizens are concerned, before going ahead with the immigration program.Our Canadian citizens should be our first concern, they are.upplyCitizenship and Immigration the ones who should get our first consideration.As for the immigrants, let us stop ourimmoderate and exaggerated publicity in Europe which leads people to believe that in Canada, they could make a livelihood without facing any responsibility.A few years ago, some Hungarians who had landed in Montreal lost no time in returning to their country because they had been misled over there before deciding to come to Canada.Mr.Chairman, I do not intend to dwell on the subject of immigration.I might say, in conclusion, that in our area immigrants behave like gentlemen.I had the opportunity on many occasions to meet in their clubs some Italian, Ukrainian, Polish and German people, and all of them, without exception, felt very much at home in our area.They are good workers who do credit to our district.We need immigrants like those, but I must stress one point: before bringing more immigrants to Canada, let us try at least to look after our own people.I would like to add a few words about Indians.The Indian affairs branch in Ottawa and the department of natural resources of the province of Quebec seem to disagree on the question whether the Indians living in northern Quebec come under the jurisdiction of Quebec or Ottawa.My good friend, the hon.member for Lapointe (Mr.Gregoire) has recently drawn the attention of the house on the small islands situated to the north of the province of Quebec and asked that these islands be granted to the province; the inhabitants of those small islands, whether Indians or Eskimos, would naturally become citizens of the province of Quebec.In northern Ungava, in the James bay area, there are also some Indians.I sincerely think that the minister is looking after their case and is cooperating with them.They need assistance.A moment ago, an hon.member said that the Indians had been the first owners of our land.He was right.The government's duty is to make it possible for the various Indian tribes to acquire as much knowledge and knowhow as possible, while retaining their own traditions and customs; their civilization may not resemble ours, but they have their own culture and education.It is up to us to help them become good citizens, people with confidence in the white man instead of fear.Indians have been taught certain aspects of history, transmitted to them from generation to generation over the years, and they have learned that three or four centuries ago, the white man has not always been very kind to them.Today, let us try to give them faith in us; they are human beings; let us help them to develop within the framework of a state.SupplyCitizenship and Immigration which should beand this is my closing remarka shining example of respect for human rights held up for the whole world to see.That example, this parliament in Ottawa, and the Canadian nation, as a whole, are quite capable of giving..

subtopic: GIFTS TO MINISTERS OF THE CROWN.
clean_text: I think that in appointing a commission of that kind some particular official, such as the deputy minister would be better qualified to inquire into matters of that kind than possibly some politician who does not know anything about technical education.I suppose this is to be put through to give a trip to the old country to four or five politicians who will bring back a report.The minister has visited these different countries, and knows something about this matter.Instead of sending such a man, probably four or five political heelers will be sent to investigate the industrial situation in these countries..

Madam Speaker, I have a few comments to make to the hon.member's submission.I invite the hon.member to point out where the government would find the authority to freeze any acquisitions that do not violate the provisions of any statute enacted by this Parliament.The government has no authority to freeze or block commercial transactions as he may be suggesting.In fact, he knows very well that his remedy is to go to his friends in the NDP in Saskatchewan.They are the ones who can remedy any matter about which he has complaints.But he will not go to those friends.He is trying to make this a political issue for the next election by simply running a smear campaign on what has been done thus far.Second, freedom of speech is guaranteed under the charter of rights and freedoms.Third, there are fundamental changes under way in methods of communications.Radio, television and now the Internet provide excellent vehicles for the exchange of information and opinion.The hon.member made reference to certain political donations.It is interesting that he never makes any comment about the political donations being made by the Bank of Nova Scotia to the NDP.The donations being made by others pale in comparison to what the Saskatchewan NDP has received from the bank and he makes these comments in the House, which are very surprising.The enforcement of the Competition Act is entrusted to the director of investigation and research who is an independent law enforcement official.His role under the merger provisions of the Competition Act is to review the economic implication and concentration of ownership.The director is not mandated to look at social issues such as editorial diversity which the NDP is pushing.

subsubtopic: ALLOTTED DAY, S O. 62-RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT-NECESSITY FOR FUNDING
clean_text: Mr.Speaker, in joining this debate let me indicate first that a year or two ago we would not have heard any discussion in the House concerning the technological revolution despite the fact that it had been taking place for approximately ten years.The Japanese have been very involved in studying the technological revolution and indicated their formal investigation and research into the matter in the Mitsubi Report of 1973.This is one of the reasons the Japanese in the 1970s became one of the dominant economies of the world.As many Hon.Members have pointed out, we are in a very historical period.We are entering the third industrial revolution.As an NDP Member pointed out, the first industrial revolution occurred when we went from an agricultural society to a society of steam engines, an industrial society, at which point 90 per cent of Canadians working in the agricultural sector lost their jobs.Those people who lost their jobs on farms went on to gain new jobs in the cities.These were jobs in the manufacturing sector or in industry in general.If we could put ourselves back into that time when Canadian society and the world society were basically agricultural, we could again sit here and be frightened about the prospects of job displacement or the fact that 90 per cent of the jobs would be displaced; or we could look with imagination at and accept the challenges ahead of us, move on and take advantage of the tremendous productivity increase and wealth generation that takes place in an industrial revolution.We have experienced this in the two previous industrial revolutions with the steam engine and then with the combustion engine.One of the first things we must overcome as Canadians is resistance to the technological revolution.This can be found in Governments, in business, in labour and consumers.The dramatic changes which have been occurring are indeed frightening.I would like to go from the general to the more specific because, as we attain knowledge and take a closer look, the fear diminishes.One of the biggest problems associated with the industrial revolution, without question, is job displacement.ay 6, 1983.and unemployment.We are hearing these days a lot of rhetorical questions and answers, seeking political points in Question Period, about the unemployment situation.Yet I believe most Hon.Members of Parliament, when they are not in the House, recognize that the majority of this unemployment is structural and is due to the technological revolution, and the best way we can handle it is with a concerted, cooperative effort.We have to deal with some pretty thorny issues and I am going to take the first one headon; that is, the deficit.I do not know how we can choose a Prime Minister, a Member of Parliament or a provincial legislator and not consider what this person's views are on the deficit.It has become very much a political question.The deficit is increasing, Government spending is increasing, let us have a return to the right, a return to "small c" conservatism.But how will that impact on our response to the technological revolutionon.Members opposite stand up and say we need further tax incentives for research and development; that we cannot destroy our social security system or our Unemployment Insurance system; that we must have more job training, as the Conservative speakers have all indicated; that we must have more retraining; that we must maintain our educational system; that we must maintain one of our most valuable resources, which is not oil but our human resources, by the best health care system in the world.The importance of a healthy population in an industrial revolution is unquestionable, but often times overlooked.However, just the areas which I have outlined now account for well over 70 per cent of the deficit.So we must deal with the situation of the deficit.Is our deficit something for which we must sacrifice the many programs which all Hon.Members are saying we need to respond to the technical revolutionOr must we put some different perspective on itet us look back at what the deficits were compared to the GNP in the United States and Canada when the first industrial revolution was going on and when the second industrial revolution was going on.Were there massive increases in investmentWere there higher Government deficits and higher business deficits in order to adapt and to modernize the industries in the first and the second industrial revolutionDid that occurYes, it did.And if Hon.Members look back at the records they will see that this is the case.This is also linked with the question of productivity.We have all heard how badly Canada is doing in terms of productivity, if you listen to much of the media or many of the Opposition Members.Quite frankly, that is not the case.We are one of the top five nations in the world in productivity.It is true, howeverand I see an Hon.Member shaking his head that our rate of increase in productivity has been diminishing.This, Mr.Speaker, is something we must address and which this Government has been addressing, particularly in the last budget.If we look over the medium and long term, we know that with a technological revolution there is a productivity multiplier effect.There is also a wealth generating effect.One of the first problems we must address is how to redistribute this.upply.ealth in such a way that those workers who have lost their jobs in the more traditional industries are able to obtain retraining in the new kinds of industries which will develop with the technological revolution.That is not just a pious hope, Mr.Speaker, this is exactly what the Japanese have done.I do not think we can point out often enough that the Japanese have gone through a major portion of a technological revolution without a dramatic increase in unemployment.I believe their unemployment rate is at about 2. per cent now.This is not because they have some hidden magic or because they are of a different culture; it is because the Japanese were aware of what was going on when they automated their automobile factories with robotics.They took the workers who were displaced and retrained them for other areas, whether in service, management or marketing, so that they did not have a rapid increase in unemployment.Perhaps they had a better opportunity to do this in their society because it is not quite as pluralistic a society as ours, and it is not quite so free a society as ours.We must face the fact that in Canada we have a number of disadvantages in terms of two levels of Government, a federal and a provincial Government..

Mr.Speaker, on October 5, Plan Nagua, a company in the riding of Beauport—Limoilou, won the 2010 Desjardins entrepreneur prize in the sustainable development category.Plan Nagua, which was created by eight students who had been on an internship in the Dominican Republic, has worked in the field of international solidarity for 40 years and is active on four fronts.It supports nearly 10,000 cooperatives in the southern hemisphere, primarily in the area of coffee growing and fair trade.It plays an active role in international cooperation projects in communities in Haiti and the Dominican Republic.It educates Quebeckers about northsouth dynamics.And it provides opportunities for international internships and equitable tourism.I am proud to salute Plan Nagua, which has sales of $2. million and spinoffs of close to $1. million in the national capital region.This company has broken new ground in Quebec in sustainable development and fair trade.

Mr.Speaker, I am excited about a new program that is having a positive impact on youth with a mobility disability in my riding and throughout New Brunswick.The Transition NB program, created by Ability New Brunswick, is helping youth with a mobility disability access training, postsecondary education, and employment..New Brunswick has the second highest rate of disability in Canada at 16.% of the population.Persons with a disability in Canada have much lower education and labour market participation rates than the general population..tudents with a disability are more likely to leave high school without a diploma compared to students without a disability, and only 13.% of persons with a disability have a university degree. Ability New Brunswick is finding creative solutions to barriers like housing, transportation, and accessibility and helping youth throughout New Brunswick reach their full abilities and potential.As today marks the start of isability Awareness Week in New Brunswick, I want to take this opportunity to thank disability organizations, their staff and volunteers for all they do—

Mr.Speaker, I am going to be splitting my time with the member for Winnipeg North.Today I am proud to speak on Bill C37, which I unreservedly support.This is an essential step in overcoming the opioid crisis that is afflicting our country.The bill amends the Customs Act and the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act, but I will actually be addressing its proposed amendments to the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act.The changes to the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act are important to our government's revision of the Canadian drugs and substances strategy, which restores harm reduction as a core pillar of Canada's drug policy.The return of this evidencebased approach to substances marks a return of our drug policy to a health matter once again.I want to acknowledge the pain that has been experienced by so many families across our country as a result of the opioid crisis.My hope is that by passing this bill, we will be preventing further deaths from the use of opioids.This bill gives health professionals the freedom to plan and implement harm reduction strategies to help people with substance abuse issues.It helps to destigmatize this disease that is taking lives every day across Canada.It will let people get medical assistance when they need it most.It is important that we all stand and support these changes.First, I will address the situation in Ontario, specifically in my community.The chief coroner for Ontario, Dr.Dirk Huyer, reports annually on deaths from opioid toxicity.If we look at the numbers, we see quickly that it is not just fentanyl that is killing people in Ontario.It is also codeine, heroin, hydromorphone, methadone, morphine, and oxycodone, sometimes mixed with alcohol..he number of deaths is rising.In 2004, there were 246 deaths from opioid and opioidalcohol toxicity.In 2015, that number had risen to 707 deaths.It is estimated that one in eight deaths of Ontarians between the ages of 25 and 34 is related to opioid use.Toronto has seen a 77% increase in overdose deaths over the past decade.The toll in east Toronto, where my community is located, has been high.Research cited by the South Riverdale Community Health Centre shows a disproportionately high number of injection drug users in our community and higher rates of emergency department visits due to opioid or cocaine use than in Toronto overall..n 2013, a memorial was unveiled at Queen St.and Carlaw Avenue in my riding.The memorial, believed to be the first of its kind in North America, helps us to remember the people in our community who have died from drug overdoses.It is a space to help families and friends heal.It encourages us to support public education and highlights the impact the war on drugs has had on the lives of people who are with us and those who have gone beyond.More than 60 people contributed to the creation of the memorial, with the guidance of artist Rocky Dobey.Regarding the memorial, he stated: .But the sculpture is only a small part of this project; many more ideas have been generated, including a print exhibit, an annual memorial at the sculpture, and the simple storytelling of memories at these meetings; hopefully the project will continue to draw this community together..t the time that it was unveiled, there were 79 names.By this summer we had 130 names, and more are being added.The stories and memories that are embodied in the sculpture should recall to all of us that work remains to be done to support our neighbours in this struggle.This past summer, the sculpture was the site of a memorial for a young community peer and street outreach worker who specialized in harm reduction, Brooklyn McNeil.She was a strong advocate for safe consumption sites in Toronto.She appeared before the Toronto Board of Health and spoke very eloquently in favour of harm reduction.I listened to her deputation last night, and her presentation hits hard.She spoke of how accidental overdoses could be prevented by safe injection sites, and she recounted her own overdose experiences.She closed her statement saying that “respect for all members of the community is so important, especially not looking at addicts as invaders but as part of the community. Unfortunately, she died of a drug overdose in June at the age of 22.She died before the Toronto Board of Health voted to approve three safe consumption sites in Toronto.. do feel that Brooklyn McNeil's view of community is echoed, however, in the deputation made by the chair of the Leslieville BIA, Andrew Sherbin, who spoke at Toronto City Hall in favour of a safe consumption site in my community at the South Riverdale Community Health Centre.He stated, “We will always be a neighbourhood that welcomes people, not turns them away. .oth of their statements strike to the very point of harm reduction, that we do not help people by turning them away.As we face a growing opioid crisis we need to look directly at this problem, we need to help people get the health care they need.The bill we are discussing today helps communities to apply for exemptions to allow for the creation of safe consumption sites.It puts into place five benchmarks to be met for a safe consumption site to be approved.The benchmarks are:.ne, demonstration of the need for such a site to exist; two, demonstration of appropriate consultation of the community; three, presentation of evidence on whether the site will impact crime in the community;our, ensuring regulatory systems are in place; and, five, site proponents will need to prove that appropriate resources are in place.By putting these benchmarks into place, the bill returns our law to the state it was in after the Supreme Court of Canada's 2011 decision that allowed lnsite to operate in British Columbia, without the overbearing, harmful, and unnecessary regulatory framework set up by the former Conservative government.An organization in my community, as I have mentioned, the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, has applied to expand the harm reduction services they already provide.The centre is one of three that was approved by the Toronto Board of Health, and it has been operating a harm reduction needle exchange since 1998.That is about 20 years.It is one of the busiest harm reduction needle exchange programs in Toronto, and in 2015 served over 3,000 people who use drugs.The South Riverdale Community Health Centre states in their background document relating to their application for a supervised injection site that international and Canadian research shows that such sites have benefits for individuals using the services and the community, including reducing the number of drug overdoses and deaths, reducing risk factors leading to infectious diseases such as HIV and hepatitis, increasing the use of detox and drug treatment services, connecting people with other health and social services, and reducing the amount of publicly discarded needles.The centre’s study of clients who seek help relating to injection drugs showed that around 30% of the clients injected in public.Ensuring needles are not discarded in public is an important health goal, and is something that this bill helps us achieve.Members of my community signed a petition in support of a safe consumption site, and the wording of the petition stated as follows:.Leslieville is a progressive, welcoming and inclusive community.As individuals who live and work in the community, we support the establishment of a smallscale safe injection service at the South Riverdale Community Health Center (SRCHC).With a 41% increase in fatal overdoses over a 10 year period in Toronto and the existence of discarded needles in the neighbourhood, this service will not only prevent unnecessary deaths but keep the community safer.South Riverdale CHC has been operating a robust and successful Harm Reduction program for almost 20 years and this small but important addition will protect both individuals who already use the program and the community at large.. would like to conclude with the comments that one of my constituents made at the Toronto Board of Health.Her name is Margaret Harvey, and she said, “As a community, we owe it to ourselves and to each other to make harm reduction a priority, to give the vulnerable a chance to get the help they need and to make our streets, parks, and other public spaces safer for everyone”..o too, as a country, do we owe it to the vulnerable to make sure that they do not face barriers to access the health care that they need to keep them safe.

Mr.Speaker, I also would have to disagree with the parliamentary secretary because I have seen the consultation that the government does, not only here but in committee.There are often members, experts, academics and other stakeholders who come to committee and offer very valuable information that can enhance the legislation before us.Time after time, the Conservatives totally reject any sort of testimony from any of the individuals, whether it is from the opposition, the experts or the other stakeholders, the very people who will be affected by legislation.We have not seen even clerical or grammar mistakes being accepted from the opposition.This is the government's record and I hope my colleague will further elaborate on that.

subtopic: FREEDOM OF SPEECH, LIBERTY OF THE PRESS, RIGHT OF ASSEMBLY
clean_text: Quite so.It is the same everywhere.And speech cannot altogether be free.I simply want to make it clear that if a member of parliament or a Canadian citizen believes there is a tendency in the application of the criminal or the civil law which will be detrimental to the country, or work against the individual or political liberties of the subject, I fail to see any other means of arousing public opinion and of bringing parliaments and legislatures to the point of amending their laws than by denouncing such judgments as they deem wrong.Now I admit it is a question of degree; it is a question of tact; it is a question of opportunity.It is very difficult to draw the line.The only point I want to make is this: in spite of any written rule of this parliament,. lay down the broad principle that under the British system of government, and preserving what is "best in that system, you cannot accomplish anything unless you take some risks, personal and political, and appeal to public opinion as against set rules, as against systems of policy, and even at times as against the authority of judges.Butand I repeat it for the second or third timeit must be done with tact and discretion; nevertheless it is very often a duty to be performed, and as regards the views held by the hon.member for Winnipeg North Centre, I think that hon.gentleman did his duty when he pointed out the danger of certain jurisprudence which had its beginning in the days of the war and which threatens to perpetuate itself.With regard to the use of foreign languages in publications or in public speech, I would point out that here again we have a very broad question of policy.I would be the last man to charge the good people of Toronto with more narrowness of mind than others.In this respect, not belonging to the province of Ontario, I am happy to repeat, and from a purely disinterested standpoint, what the hon.member for South Toronto has said.As an individual I can render testimony to the breadth of mind of a large proportion of the individuals of all classes inhabiting the city of Toronto.This may surprise my good neighbour (Mr.Neill), who seems horrified.I know some of my good Ontario Grit friends would not agree with that, but I claim to be a living witness to the fact.I believe I have denounced the views, political and social, held by the majority of the people of Toronto, in the city of Toronto, and before various bodiesuniversities and clubsmore often perhaps than any other member of this house.Freedom of Speech.

Madam Speaker, I am pleased, as a member of the Bloc Quebecois, to speak on Bill C71 and more specifically on Motion No.1 introduced by the hon.member for SaintHyacinthe—Bagot.The budget is always an important issue, though it may be dry.But it is very important, in particular for Quebecers who give the federal government $30 billion in taxes every year, without being sure of reap all the due benefits from this annual collective effort, which is a huge effort on the part of Quebecers.It cannot be said that the federal government is greatly involved in Quebecers' everyday lives.However, if there is one area where it does get involved, it is employment insurance.What the federal government in recent years has been appalling, not to say dramatic, for thousands and thousands of Quebecers and Canadians.In 1990, about 90% of those who contributed to employment insurance were eligible for benefits when they had the misfortune—because it is a misfortune, something those on the other side and specifically the Minister of the Human Resources Development tend to forget—to lose their job.We should be there as a community and as a government to support those individuals who have to go through such dire straits.In 1990, about 90% of those who contributed to insurance employment had the good fortune or privilege to receive benefits.Then the Conservatives began to fiddle with employment insurance, and the Liberals merrily carried on.Yet, the members opposite, who were then in the opposition, had condemned this.In spite of that—and this is typical—the government is still fiddling and only 36% of those who contribute to employment insurance are now eligible for benefits.This, as we know, allowed the government to eliminate the deficit at the expense of low income taxpayers, low income earners and the most vulnerable people.The government took care to protect the interests of the wealthy.It has always found a way to justify the existence of tax shelters and, most of all, tax havens.Since the Liberals took office, there has been one scandal here, in Ottawa, in the ConservativeLiberal tradition.I am talking about the family trust scandal that allowed a well known family to avoid paying somewhere between $400 and $700 million in federal taxes.These are not mere details.This happened in spite of the fact that the government and particularly the Department of Finance were fully aware of what was going on and in spite of the fact that the Auditor General of Canada expressed his disagreement before being put in his place by the accounting gurus, in particular those who develop tax schemes in Toronto.The auditor general—and I witnessed it personally—was simply told to mind his own business by these thinkers from Toronto who protect the interests of the rich in Canada and who develop these tax schemes to help them.This budget has also created a precedent in Canadian history in that the rules of the game have been changed with regard to federal transfers to the provinces.The government has decided that, from now on, these transfers will be made on a per capita basis according to the population of each province instead of being based on the needs of each province.It is a fundamental change and the results are brilliant.This means that, over the next five years, Ontario will get 47% of these transfers, whereas Quebec will get 8%.With this kind of management, it is no wonder Quebecers want out.It is one reason among many.On of the reasons Quebecers want out and will be increasingly eager to get out—and this budget is part of that Canadian process—is the social union framework agreement, which is a major event in Canadian history, even though its name does not appear to mean much.The term social union is a rather insignificant one, one that does not generate much discussion, but does imply an enormous impact on the new Canada of tomorrow, the new Canada of the year 2000 and beyond, the new Canada built here in Ottawa, which will be centralized and unitary.The provinces have recently given the federal government legal authorization to intervene in areas in which they have sole jurisdiction: health, education and social programs.These, according to the 1867 constitution, which we respect, were the exclusive jurisdiction—important words—of the provinces.With that recent consent by the provinces, the federal government has been given authorization to get involved in these areas in order to turn this country in the future into a centralized and unitary country, one in which decisions will be made—efficiently, let us hope—so that it can have a competitive edge internationally, where it has been rather lagging behind until now.This new Canada is looking for ways to be competitive, but everyone will pay a price as far as structure is concerned.This means that, slowly but surely, the provincial governments will find themselves turning into regional governments, with considerably diminished powers.This means that Quebec is not only a province, but one just like the others.This means that the Quebec people has never been recognized as a distinct entity by the rest of Canada, and never will be.There is no such thing as a Quebec people in the context of a new unitary Canada.Such notions will have no place in the new Canada of tomorrow.This means that there will be national standards in all sectors in which the federal government has a hand, including education, which was what Jean Charest, former leader of the Progressive Conservative Party of Canada and now leader of the Quebec Liberal Party, argued for in 1997.There will be national standards to evaluate students and schools from coast to coast.This is what the Canada of tomorrow will look like, with national standards set by Ottawa applying to all the provinces, which will become regional governments.In my opinion, Canada has to centralize because of international pressure.Countries dealing with Canada want clearer rules.They want to know if there are disagreements between the provinces and the federal government.The government is in the process of clearing the way, without debate, for the federal government to have a free hand to sign whatever contracts it wishes, particularly international ones, legitimately and with full jurisdiction.This is a necessity for Canada, but it is a disaster for Quebec.If Quebecers remain in Canada, they will slowly disappear.It is Louisiana all over again.As the member for SaintMaurice said, and it was perhaps the only time he showed any vision in his entire career, Quebec would be a larger version of New Brunswick.If Quebec decided to remain within the new structure of the Canada of tomorrow, it would become a larger version of New Brunswick.All we Quebecers must ask ourselves if this is the kind of Canada in which we want to live, or whether it would not be simpler for Quebec to govern itself the way it wished, and Canada to do the same, and for both parties to enjoy the best possible relations that the good neighbours we have always been can have.I hope that our fellow Quebecers will give some thought to this very important development in the history of Canada.

Mr.Speaker, I welcome the opportunity to speak in this debate.Since the early eighties when I was the Minister of Employment and Immigration I felt that the way in which this country deals with those thousands of people victimized around the world is one of our most important responsibilities.It used to be said that a country is determined by how it treats its least fortunate.In this world of interdependence I think we are as much judged by how we treat the least fortunate around the world.In that case, I can only say that this bill we are debating in its final reading is a tale of tragedy.First, there is the tragedy of the people we are dealing with.The numbers of refugees who appear on our screens each night on the news are only a small sample of the enormous misfortune and suffering experienced by tens of thousands of men, women and children around the world.There is a tendency in this country to think that somehow we are threatened by these movements of people..ovember 24, 1992.overnment Orders.e are very privileged because if you look at what is happening today in Germany, Croatia or Afghanistan, they are host countries that are accepting hundreds of thousands of refugees.The kind of drumbeat of fear and concern that is being raised by certain sources in this country is really one of illusion.The other tragedy that this debate recommends is what we are doing about it.The bill in front of us represents a major change in the governing philosophy this country has used since the end of the Second World War to deal with these mass movements of people who have been victimized abroad.It has been one of the important acts of stewardship of the Parliament of Canada to ensure that Canada has an open door policy and not a closed door policy.This bill almost caps what I have seen over the past several years as a major turning away from that philosophy.It is a major shift in the attitude toward immigrants and refugees as if somehow we have turned them into a scapegoat, something to be feared and something to be cautioned against, as opposed to saying something that is part of our responsibility as a privileged nation to provide for open and active reception.Just think for a moment that at the end of the Second World War Canada accepted hundreds of thousands of displaced persons who had been thrown up in the turbulence and turmoil of the Second World War.There were hundreds of thousands of Canadians, many of whose sons and daughters are sitting in the House of Commons today because they got that chance.Think for a moment what happened during the time of the major revolt in Czechoslovakia and in Hungary in the mid1950s when again Canada opened its doors to tens of thousands of Hungarians, Czechs and Poles who were escaping communist tyranny.We are not talking about small hundreds, we are talking about tens of thousands.Think of what happened when Idi Amin took over Uganda and expelled the East Indians who had been part of that country for generations.Again, this country was a leader in accepting tens of thousands of people who had been ethnically cleansed from Uganda at the time.Think of the record that was jointly shared by both the previous Clark government and the Trudeau government, accepting over 200,000 refugees from IndoChina.There were 200,000 in contrast with what happened in this House of Commons when 26 refugees arrived on the shore of Nova Scotia.We were called back into emergency session of Parliament because it was portrayed by the then minister of immigration as being a national emergency that somehow Canada was going to be invaded by this great hoard.If that was not a clear signal of the changes that we are now beginning to see the final fruits of in this bill, I do not know what was.It was a clear sense that all of a sudden the government had broken with that continuity of policy.It had begun to take a totally different attitude.It had begun to go back to the days that Irving Abella wrote about in the 1930s in her book None is too Many when the philosophy of the Canadian government was how many to keep out and not how many to bring in.It was an exclusionary policy based upon sheer discrimination.Sadly, Bill C86 only brings to a final stage that kind of Draconian, discriminatory closed door attitude that has since become the new hallmark of Canadian immigration refugee policy.It is so ironic and sad in a way to hear the Minister of Employment and Immigration and other members on the other side of the House talk with some pride about the honours that have been given to Canada for our work dealing with refugees, taking a lead.It is as if somehow this justified what they are doing now without recognizing that the bill that we are now debating contradicts and rejects the very fundamental premises upon which Canada acquired that sense of reputation and distinction.It is turning its back on those previous positions and policies and philosophies that were taken by both Liberal and Conservative governments over the past 45 years.When the record of this government is written, which will be done in the next six or seven weeks, when the historians finally get a chance to sift through the sand and find out what it did, this will be one of the blackest marks on the record of this government.It is basically saying that rather than responding in an open, generous and progressive way to the incredible problems of dis.ovember 24, 1992.placed persons and refugees, of victims of tyranny and economic deprivation, it is withdrawing inside the fortress.I admit we are not alone.There is no question that is the dominant response by the various neoconservative governments in the western world, and we are seeing it.Look what happened in Britain a week ago.It refused to take people who had been kicked out of their country in Bosnia.Is our record any better recall with great force the announcements of the minister last summer about accepting 25,000 families from the horrible situation in former Yugoslavia; great fanfare, great announcement, great public photo opportunities, but if you look at the record today, about 150 to 200 families have been accepted.It is disgraceful, absolutely disgraceful.We see what is happening in Somalia with the incredible human suffering and when representation and pressure were made to see if Canada could bring out particularly orphaned children and others, the response of the Minister of Employment and Immigration was to the effect that they really would not fit into Canada.Never mind the big cases that hit the newspapers, look what has happened.You can go to any office of any constituency of any member of Parliament in this country and they can tell you what is going on in the Department of Employment and Immigration in terms of exercising some judgment based upon compassion or humanitarianism, looking at it case by case to see if there is some kind of judgment call that could be made as the responsibility of the minister.I recall when I was in that post, Friday nights when I left Ottawa to go back to Winnipeg with two briefcases full of cases to look at, it was the most awesome and heavy responsibility I have ever had in my life because I was making literally judgments about life and death for so many people.The reason the minister was given discretion in the Immigration Act of 1977 was that some human judgment based upon a sense of fairness or equity or compassion could be exercised.That has ceased to exist.There is no judgment being made, it has been turned into an assembly line.The assembly line is reject, reject, reject.There is no compassion being exercised, there is no sense of justice being used.Government Orders.he standard formula now is "no point going to the minister's office, it does not care.We know that we will simply get the black dot, the rejection slip".Look what it is doing.The latest announcement is it is going to set up new immigration processing centres in Vegreville.Vegreville is a great place to have it but are we turning our immigration refugee procedure into an assembly line in which you run the paper through as if it were some kind of massproduced washing machineWe are talking about human beings here, we are talking about people's livespeople who need the kind of interface with an immigration officer; talking to families, listening to their cases, having officials know there is a philosophy to judge as to whether that case deserves some sort of fair treatment.Now we are turning it into a paragon of efficiency.Even at that, the government is downsizing the department by 25 per cent.Increasingly there will be less and less human contact and the long lines of refugees sitting in the camps in eastern Europe, Africa, Asia and Latin America will never see anybody again.The long lines of people trying to reunite with their families from the Philippines or India will never get to have their applications even considered by the human eye.Then we get Bill C86 on which my colleague from NotreDamedeGrace waged a wonderful rearguard action trying to hold back this juggernaut that is moving against us.The government attempts to justify it as a way of getting better control.It is fooling people but it is not telling the truth.The socalled third safe country principleIf someone has come from a country that Canada considers a proper country of good bona fide credentials then they automatically get rejected at the border under this legislation.How do we explain that for long periods of time the United States of America under the Reagan and Bush administrations rejected close to 85 per cent or 90 per cent of all refugee applicants from Central America, especially during the turmoils in Nicaragua, El Salvador and now in Guatemala.For ideological reasons the Government of the United States would not accept that someone fleeing terrorism or the death squad in Central America was a bona fide refugee because the government of America was supporting those regimes..overnment Orders.nder this legislation we have to send them back to the United States, which means they go back into the cauldron.They go back where their lives and their families are threatened and endangered.How can we say that is a criteria that must be honoured not as an exception but as the standard rule with no right of appealLook at the story that came out last week.It was I think again a sign of the times.Dr.Sam Ifejika, a member of the refugee board, resigned because he said: "I have been asked to explain my positive decisions a number of times but I have never been asked to explain my negative decisions.The direction of the refugee board is against the letter and spirit of the United Nations convention on refugees and Ottawa's proposed new immigration law would foster this inhuman policy".This is their own appointment.This is a member of their own board who resigned because he said in effect the whole weight of government policy is: How do you close the door and keep people outt really surprises me because this has not been the tradition of the Conservative Party.I recall very well when I became the successor to the minister of immigration, Mr.Atkey, when he said one of the responsibilities of a minister of immigration is to make sure not to open up the Pandora's box of fear and intimidation about refugees and immigration.Yet that has been the underlying philosophy with this government since the day it came in.It has broken that continuity and it has turned its back on traditions that have been established successively through the St.Laurent, Pearson, Diefenbaker, Trudeau and Clark governments and I am not sure why.I guess we could all speculate as to what would be the reasons for this shift, this major transformation.I can tell hon.members and I think all my colleagues who are in the House with me would agree that it will not last very long.When there is an election and the people of this country change the government, what is now Bill C86 will be the law for only a short period of time.This is not the way we would deal with immigrants and refugees around the world.We cannot accept that Canada should so clearly turn its face against its responsibilities.Let us not quibble about that.All those bureaucrats and people who are getting the engine running and putting the pieces together had better make it temporary because it is not going to last that long.There will be a change back to the far more humane and responsible Liberal notion of what to do.This government may have some shortterm satisfaction that it has shown the Reform Party it can be tougher on immigrants than the Reform Party can be, but it is a false victory that will not last any longer than this government will last.While I have the opportunity, let me just say that another part of the picture is missing.The amendments proposed by my colleague were very important.He said that one of the things which has to be done about the whole area of immigration and refugee policy is to make it more democratic and more accountable.Appointments to the board should be approved by Parliament.Regulations tabled must be put in a way that they can be not only examined but also rejected by parliamentary vote.There must be a far more open system of examination so that it is transparent and visible.We have also put forward that if there is going to be a proper refugee immigration policy there must also be deep concern for what happens after people arrive here.What are the present figuresI think over half of those who come into this country do not receive proper education in French or English.It is no wonder they become a problem when they are not even given one of the basic tools to survive, which is one of the languages of the land.The Government of Canada continues to shrink the second language programs in this country.I will not have time to fully get into this and it may be a debate for another time, but there must also be an international response.We cannot pretend that we can solve these problems simply by ourselves.If Canada surely wanted to show leadership in this world, this is an area that we could take on as our own to try to bring the countries of a developed, industrial world, which have wealth and advantage and privilege, to ask: How do we deal with the vast amounts of human sufferingHow do we make sure people will not be freezing in the cold of Bosnia this winter, or dying of starvation in Somalia, or being left to neglect in Afghanistan for tens of years, or.ovember 24, 1992.being left hanging around the camps in the Middle East as they have been for the last 40 or 50 yearsurely this area has been neglected by all the countries, but because of our tradition and reputation we can provide an important contribution to the world by drawing attention at the G7 and at the United Nations population conferences for a brand new definition of what is a refugee and how the world comes to grips with an enormous and gargantuan, but singularly human, problem.I say that we cannot take on that kind of leadership ourselves until we undo this kind of legislation which sets Canada back 75 years..

Mr.Speaker, I truly do appreciate the opportunity to participate in this debate tonight along with many of my colleagues in the House.As so many of them have indicated tonight, I have also been following the situation in Ukraine very closely over the past number of months.I can say that our government is deeply disappointed with the Ukrainian government's decision to suspend signing of the association agreement and the deep and comprehensive free trade area with the European Union.Like hundreds of thousands of Ukrainian citizens, we believe the decision to not sign the association agreement represents a lost opportunity.We stand with all Ukrainians who are fighting for their beliefs in a democratic European Ukraine, and we believe that Ukraine's best hope for democracy and economic prosperity lies in closer alignment with European and North American norms and institutions..ore disturbing is the shady way in which the new laws were passed by the Ukrainian parliament on January 16.If they stand, these new laws will place serious limitations on the human rights and civil liberties of Ukrainians.In particular, we are very concerned about a new measure that would define Ukrainian nongovernmental or civil society organizations as “foreign agents” if they receive money or property from foreign countries and participate in any socalled political activities.The new law would require such foreign agents to register with the government and to provide monthly reports on their activities and income.They would also be subject to different tax treatments on these foreign funds.These changes could be a serious hindrance to the economic prosperity of the Ukrainian people and would have a serious impact on Canada's development assistance work in the Ukraine.As a way of providing some context for this debate, I would like to take a few moments to tell members about Canada's work to help Ukraine achieve economic prosperity.As members know, the historic ties between our two nations extend back through generations of Ukrainian migration to Canada.For more than 120 years, Ukrainianborn Canadians have contributed to the social, economic and political fabric of Canada.Today, Ukrainians make up almost four per cent of the total Canadian population.I know my riding of Elmwood—Transcona is very reflective of this..conomically, Canada and Ukraine enjoy positive commercial relationships, with twoway trade that totalled $313. million in 2012.Over the years, we have developed a close bilateral relationship, a solid economic partnership, and strong peopletopeople ties.In 1991, Canada was the first western nation to recognize Ukraine's independence.Since then, much of our development assistance in Ukraine has focused on increasing economic opportunities for Ukrainians in a strengthened democracy.Over the years, Canadian development assistance investments in privatesector development and governance in Ukraine have contributed to the country's transition from a centrally planned system toward a freemarket democratic model.However, Ukraine was an integral part of the former Soviet Union, and as such its economic transition has been much slower and more difficult.To build resilience and achieve broadbased prosperity, Ukraine must diversify and grow its real economy, especially through developing its small and mediumenterprise sector, which is far smaller than in other European countries.Stimulating the growth of small and medium enterprises will also help to expand and strengthen the middle class.We know from experience that a healthy, civically engaged middle class and smallbusiness sector will help to nurture a wellfunctioning democracy.Given its rich natural resources, reasonable labour costs and large and welleducated population, Ukraine has excellent economic potential.However, it will face challenges in becoming competitive.If concluded, planned free trade agreements with Europe and Canada will help to provide a road map to greater competitiveness within a predictable rulesbased framework. To increase rates of economic growth in Ukraine, Canada is focusing on three areas of intervention.The first area is to strengthen the investment climate in a sustainable way by building economic foundations.In practical terms, this means improving the capacity of all levels of government, including local governments, to deliver on the basic needs of citizens and create a supportive framework for local business growth and trade and investment.That means providing security and a level playing field for small and medium enterprises, from fair and transparent regulations, to independent and predictable application of the rule of law.The second area of focus is growing businesses, especially those that are micro, small, and mediumsized firms, including those in the agricultural sector.Our goal is to help make these businesses more sustainable and competitive.We would do this by helping entrepreneurs access the things we take for granted in Canada, such as business networks, value chains, productivityenhancing technology, insurance and business financing.The third area of focus, and one I believe in strongly for a number of reasons, is investing in people, especially women and youth.Our objective is to build a skilled, trained workforce of women and men who can seize opportunities in a rapidly expanding labour market that is fuelled by the needs of local and international employers.Careful investment of development assistance in these areas will lead to increased employment opportunities and enhanced business productivity and profitability in Ukraine, resulting in rising household incomes and reduced poverty over the long term.This is also in Canada's interest.As Ukraine's economy continues to grow, so will the economic ties between our two countries.Canada's development program has contributed significantly to advancing Ukraine's sustainable economic growth.Agriculture is a key growth sector for the Ukraine.Canadian support has helped to increase the competitiveness of smallholder fruit, vegetable, and dairy farmers who have invested in improved technology and are working together to market highervalue, higherquality products demanded by the marketplace.With help from Canada and specialized technical assistance from Israel, smallholder horticulture farmers in southern Ukraine have sold more than 12,000 tons of produce, for over $12 million.Most importantly, they have increased their household income by over 30% since the start of this project.Agricultural insurance is an essential component of a modern agricultural economy.It allows farmers to invest with confidence and for banks to lend to farmers with confidence.With support from Canada, Ukraine has implemented a new agricultural insurance system based on international best practices.At the start of 2013, seven Ukrainian insurance companies were selling the 14 products developed by this project, resulting in more than 1,000 insurance contracts covering a total of 1.29 million hectares of crop.The crop area that is covered expands every day.Canada is also helping to create partnerships between Canadian and Ukrainian cities to help them modernize their approach to local economic development planning.The Federation of Canadian Municipalities has worked with municipal partners in 12 cities to develop and implement municipal strategic development plans.These plans have helped to operationalize 15 demonstration projects, in tourism development, city branding and marketing, and business centre creation.As part of the implementation of the cities' strategic plans, an additional 45 local economic development projects were launched.Cities were able to attract $80 million in investments from private and donor sectors, thanks to a training program for city officials on how to promote cities' competitive advantages.We are working to improve the planning and delivery of services that support economic growth at the municipal level.We have also helped government institutions at the regional level to formulate and implement reformoriented policies that are in the public interest and adhere to international best practices.We are assisting with the development of a national demanddriven vocational skills training systems across the country.Each of these initiatives will help to increase broadbased economic growth in Ukraine.We are doing these things because we believe in Ukraine and its people.As I mentioned earlier, we are very concerned that our partners not be deemed foreign agents.That would subject them to an additional taxation and reporting burden, limiting their ability to achieve meaningful results for the Ukrainian people.It may even force some of them to close their shops.We have strong historic ties with Ukraine.We have forged strong alliances out of a shared understanding of Ukraine's unique challenges, as well as a common desire to see the country overcome them.We look forward to continuing with that work and to helping Ukraine and Ukrainians achieve the freedom and prosperity they not only yearn for but that they deserve.We remain hopeful that the current crisis will come to a peaceful settlement and that Ukraine will begin to get its economy back on track.In summary, Canada is deeply concerned about the deterioration of democracy and rule of law in Ukraine.Canada remains committed to helping Ukraine by working to improve economic opportunities for Ukrainians in a strengthened democracy.Canadian assistance is also focused on facilitative judicial and civil service reforms.Canada's support for small and medium enterprise growth is helping to develop a middle class in the Ukraine, which is essential for ensuring a sustainable democratic transition.We are monitoring the situation very closely and will continue to deliver assistance as appropriate.Canada will continue to stand by Ukrainians who are expressing their concern with the recent direction their government has undertaken.

Mr.Speaker, I should like to associate myself with those who have spoken in suppport of the resolution introduced by the hon.member for Mount Royal (Mr.Macnaughton).It is a worthwhile resolution and should be accepted.In speaking to the resolution, I should like to say that this investigation should embrace the causes of mental illness, the problems that are effected by it and the treatment of it.The investigation should be organized in that order.As we.all know, mental health comes under the jurisdiction of the provinces.However, it should be organized by the provinces in cooperation with the federal government.As to the causes of mental illness, there are many.There are congenital causes which can be inherited, illnesses can be caused by birth injuries, and some may be due to environment.They can also be due to the illnesses of the parents, which are not their fault or which might be their fault.The cause may be alcoholism, drug addiction, physical illness, social disease or other causes.Children who come from such homes as I have mentioned should not be blamed.It could not possibly be their fault and the children should not have to suffer for the sins of the parents.Treatment should be available to all these people.Investigation should be made into treatment that would cure them.I understand that the line between those who are mentally retarded or mentally handicapped and those who are not is very thin indeed.It should not be impossible for the medical profession to find ways and means of solving those problems.The problems looking toward a cure are, of course, educational, constitutional and financial.However, the financial problem should not be a matter of too great moment because if the governments fulfil their obligations the financial strain or stress should not be too much on the private individual.The matters of treatment could, of course, embrace mental hygiene.So far as retarded children are concerned there is always a way of training them.There are certain people with cerebral palsy and other afflictions who can get training and treatment throughout their lives that will equip them to do certain things, even though the wall between retardation and intelligence is not too great.Of course, for adults there are the mental institutions and general hospitals.As I said before, there should be very little difficulty in the medical profession in finding ways and means of overcoming the defects that these unfortunate people suffer from.In conclusion, I, along with a great many others here today, would like to recommend to the minister that this resolution be accepted and approved.It would give me great pleasure to see this happen..
